After dropping in the NPI poll to end the regular season, Penn State’s first-round battle with the Golden Gophers suddenly had sizable implications for its shot at a second-straight NCAA Tournament appearance.

The Nittany Lions’ strong quarterfinals win, fueled by a dominant team performance, helped to increase those chances and build momentum as their playoff run continues, however.

The return of star forward Charlie Cerrato was vital for Penn State’s postseason push and has already made a considerable impact.

After the sophomore went down with an injury back in January, he made his return to the lineup in the blue and white’s final regular-season game, registering an assist in the 5-2 loss against Wisconsin.

Cerrato also opened the scoring to tally his first goal in over two months in the Nittany Lions’ quarterfinals win, and will play a crucial role in the team’s final push.

Penn State will also look to freshman phenoms Gavin McKenna and Jackson Smith as it advances, both of whom registered points in Wednesday’s battle.

McKenna, a current candidate for the Hobey Baker award, is ranked second nationally in points per game.

The forward’s multi-point performance in the first round solidified him in the blue and white history books as the lone freshman to tally 50 points in a single season, and is only the third skater in program history to achieve the feat.

Smith, the 14th overall draft pick in this year’s NHL draft, broke the record for most single-season goals by a defenseman in program history back in February and has been instrumental in Penn State’s success thus far.

Scouting Michigan

The second-round matchup marks the fifth time this season that the Wolverines and the Nittany Lions will meet. Michigan holds the edge in the season series between the conference foes, 2-1-1.

Michigan finished the regular season with a 26-7-1 record and is currently the top-ranked team in the USCHO poll.

The Wolverines defeated Notre Dame in their own quarterfinals matchup, 6-1, behind the efforts of five different scorers to advance in the conference tournament.

Behind McKenna, Michigan has two of the top three scorers in the Big Ten in Michael Hage and T.J. Hughes. Hage has tallied 49 points in 35 games, while Hughes is right behind with 48, and both forwards have had strong performances when facing the blue and white this season.

Hage has seven assists in the two teams’ previous meetings, while Hughes has registered three goals and three assists.

In net, the Wolverines also have one of the top netminders in the country in Jack Ivankovic.

The 2025 second-round NHL draft pick to Nashville has posted a .923 save percentage and a 2.113 goals against average this season, playing a crucial part in the Wolverines’ success.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Column | Grading each Penn State men’s basketball player following 2025-26 season
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Heading into the 2025-26 season, Penn State had the youngest roster in the Big Ten and was picked to finish last in the conference by the preseason media poll.

That prediction held true as the overall inexperienced roster ended at 12-20 overall and 3-17 in Big Ten play to clinch last place in the conference standings during Mike Rhoades’ third year at the helm of the program.

Moving forward, which players remaining with the Nittany Lions for 2026-27 will be an important part of the offseason for Penn State, so here’s an assessment and a grade for each player on the roster.

Kayden Mingo

Guard Kayden Mingo wasn’t included on the Big Ten All-Freshman Team, but he played like a rookie who deserved the honor this season. Across 28 appearances, he averaged 13.7 points, 3.5 rebounds, 4.3 assists and 2.1 steals per game with 47.7% shooting from the field.

Mingo is an aggressive two-way player who can drive in to score baskets and give a relentless effort on the defensive end. He scored the game-winning shot against Minnesota on Feb. 1, and he had 16 multi-steal performances this season, including eight steals versus Sacred Heart on Nov. 29, which tied a single-game school record and helped him garner Big Ten Freshman of the Week honors.

The Farmingdale, New York, native is a must-retain player for the Nittany Lions, and he alluded to a return following the team’s loss to Northwestern in the first round of the Big Ten Tournament on Tuesday.

Mingo needs to become a better shooter as he shot 24.4% from 3-point range this season. Still, if you’re Penn State, you really couldn’t ask for much more out of the freshman, who had a productive first season playing as the Nittany Lions’ highest-rated recruit of all time.

Grade: A

Freddie Dilione V

On the one hand, guard Freddie Dilione V’s shot creation is a work of art that helped him become Penn State’s leading scorer this season. On the other hand, Dilione can be a liability on defense at times as he doesn’t always seem to be a great fit in Rhoades’ scheme.

Dilione averaged a career-high 14.0 points per game with 47.3% shooting from the field across 32 appearances, including 15 starts. When he gets the ball, he typically finds his spot near the free-throw line and knocks down a mid-range jumper.

However, his inconsistent defense seems to at least be partly why he wasn’t always in the starting lineup. It took until Dec. 29 against NC Central for Dilione to make his first start of the season, and he came off the bench in the season-finale versus Northwestern.

The redshirt junior’s offensive talents makes him an intriguing player for Penn State to keep around for next season. Dilione could also opt to enter the transfer portal to spend his last year of eligibility elsewhere.

Regardless, Dilione’s offensive production prevents his defense from making a big hit to his grade.

Grade: B

Ivan Jurić

Freshman Ivan Jurić showcased a ton of promise as a 7-footer in the Big Ten this season. He averaged 10.2 points and 5.3 rebounds per game with 57.7% shooting from the field across 30 appearances.

Jurić has the usual skillset for a paint presence with his ability to back down defenders for layups, but what makes him an even more intriguing player is that he has a 3-point shot in his arsenal. He made 15-of-53 shots from 3-point range, including 2-of-5 performances against both Michigan State and Michigan.

Furthermore, Jurić ended the year on a tear, highlighted by his career-high 22 points against Northwestern. Across the last five games of the season, he averaged 15.4 points and 6.2 rebounds per game with 70.7% shooting from the field.

Sure, Jurić lacks a shot-blocking trait and needs to be better at boxing out on rebounds and foul trouble at times, but he’s a 7-footer who has the potential to play inside and out and is only a freshman. Another offseason of development should make him into an even better player for next year, as he’s also a must-retain player.

Grade: A-

Josh Reed

Forward Josh Reed transferred to Penn State last offseason after spending three years at Cincinnati, and that decision allowed him to have the best individual season of his career.

Reed averaged 11.5 points and 4.1 rebounds per game, 48.8% shooting from the field across 32 appearances, including 31 starts. For reference, Reed was never a consistent starter with the Bearcats, and his previous best averages came last season with 4.8 points and 2.5 rebounds per game with 41% shooting from the field.

Down the stretch of this year, Reed arguably became Penn State’s most reliable player. He scored in double figures in 16 of the last 18 games of the season, and he shot 47.1% from 3-point range during those games.

In addition to scoring a career-high 25 points to help the Nittany Lions defeat Iowa on Feb. 28, Reed was a quality defender and gave a great effort to box out. Reed should be the standard for the type of production Penn State finds out of veteran players in the transfer portal.

Grade: A

Dominick Stewart

Guard Dominick Stewart was given credit for his defense on All-Big Ten first team guard Bennett Stirtz during Penn State’s win over Iowa, and his defensive capabilities are seemingly why he started 24 games for the Nittany Lions this season.

However, he wasn’t a good enough shooter for someone who took the volume of shots Stewart did. In 32 appearances this season, Stewart averaged 6.6 points, 2.2 rebounds and 1.6 assists per game with 34.8% shooting from 3-point range.

To be frank, Penn State likely would’ve earned an upset over then-No. 2 Michigan on Jan. 6 had Stewart been a better shooter. Stewart had numerous open opportunities from beyond the arc, but he finished with 1-of-7 shooting from deep as the Nittany Lions lost, 74-72.

It’s tough to overlook the struggles of Stewart, who also had some untimely turnovers or fouls as he committed 76 fouls, which was the second highest on the team. Stewart had 20 games with one or zero 3-pointers made, and he shot 29.4% from three in Big Ten games.

Grade: C+

Eli Rice

Wing Eli Rice, who’s known as a shooter, once ranked among the top in the Big Ten for 3-point percentage this season, but since the start of February, he has only made two 3-pointers as he became less efficient down the stretch. He didn’t make a 3-point shot in the last five games of the regular season.

Rice averaged 6.7 points and 1.7 rebounds per game across 29 appearances and 18 starts this season, and he shot 39% from 3-point range. In conference play, Rice only shot 34.7% from three, and he was benched in the Big Ten Tournament game against Northwestern.

He also wasn’t always a reliable defender, so when his shooting was on a cold streak, he saw fewer minutes than usual. During four of the last five games of the regular season, Rice only played 12.5 minutes per game despite averaging 19.8 minutes per game this season.

Grade: C-

Melih Tunca

Throughout the first eight games of the season, guard Melih Tunca scored in double figures each time, including a season-high 20 points in a win over La Salle. Since then, however, Tunca has only reached double digits five times.

Tunca was an intriguing international addition from Turkey, as he had professional experience in the Turkish Basketball Super League, which is the top professional league in his home country. Despite that, he didn’t always seem ready for the Big Ten, as he saw less action in conference play due to defensive struggles.

Additionally, Tunca managed to knock down just 23.1% of his 3-pointers in Big Ten play. For reference, he made 47.5% of his 3-pointers in the 2024-25 season while playing in the Turkish Basketball Super League.

The potential of Tunca can be seen, and he showed an ability to make tough layups in the paint, particularly at the beginning of the season. I’d say it’s still worth it to keep Tunca around for another season despite not having as much production this year as I would’ve expected.

Grade: C

Mason Blackwood

Forward Mason Blackwood made his first career start in the Big Ten Tournament game against Northwestern in place of Rice. As Rhoades put it postgame, he played well defensively but needs more than zero points on offense.

Blackwood getting the start was seemingly a reward for his efforts, as Rhoades said he was playing harder than others in the three weeks leading up to the game. Blackwood giving full effort was a trend throughout the season, showcasing promise in his freshman year.

He didn’t always see the most playing time in games, but Blackwood flashed his potential in extended looks, recording 14 points and six rebounds with 57.1% shooting in 22 minutes against Wisconsin on Jan. 22.

Blackwood already has a Big Ten frame for a wing at 6-foot-7 and 230 pounds. His averages this season were 2.6 points and 1.8 rebounds in 12.2 minutes per game across 26 appearances, but he’s sneakily a must-retain player as I could easily see him as a consistent starter next season.

Grade: A-

Tibor Mirtič

Forward Tibor Mirtič was injured for half of the season, missing the last 17 games of the year. But when he was on the court, he provided some solid production for a rotational big man who was only playing 14.1 minutes per game.

Out of 15 games played, Mirtič scored at least two field goals in nine of them. He shot 53.2% from the field, and if two outlier performances against Navy and Pitt are excluded — he shot a combined 0-of-7 across both games — he shot 62.5% from the field this season.

Mirtič battled for boards as well, and his 22 offensive rebounds are fourth highest on the team, even though he dealt with injuries and missed games. He needs to improve from a 30.8% free-throw shooter, but it’s worth it to keep Mirtič around in a similar role next season.

Grade: B

Justin Houser

As mentioned before, the original plan for freshman 7-footer Justin Houser was to potentially redshirt this season and develop behind 7-footer Yanic Konan Niederhauser. However, with Konan Niederhauser leaving for the 2025 NBA draft, Houser got a chance to play this year.

Houser, who averaged 1.4 points and 1.1 rebounds per game across 18 appearances, is a raw prospect, but that didn’t stop him from showing some promise. Despite playing the second-least amount of minutes this season, Houser had a team-high eight blocks, including three blocks against USC on Feb. 8, as his rim protection and effort were on display.

Houser still has another offseason or two of development until he can turn into a more productive player in the rotation for Penn State. That showed at times this season with fouling issues, but Houser earning meaningful minutes this early on in his career should be considered a quality Year 1.

Grade: B

Saša Ciani

Penn State added forward Saša Ciani out of the transfer portal last summer so it could have a veteran big man with collegiate experience on the roster. In the 2024-25 season, Ciani played at UIC, where he averaged 9.9 points and 7.6 rebounds per game with 53.3% shooting from the field.

However, Ciani’s first season with the Nittany Lions didn’t work out from a production standpoint as he took a backseat to Jurić’s breakout. After starting the first four games of the season, Ciani came off the bench in the last 24 games he played, averaging 2.1 points and 1.9 rebounds per game.

Ciani didn’t showcase much touch near the rim, shooting 38.9% from the field despite taking mostly short-range shots. Since the UCLA game on Jan. 14, Ciani has only made two baskets and never had more than three rebounds in a game.

Grade: F

Reggie Grodin

Guard Reggie Grodin didn’t play this season as it was a redshirt year for the freshman. He was a 3-star prospect in the 2025 class out of The Newman School in Massachusetts.

Grade: N/A

Chris Lotito

Forward Chris Lotito is Penn State’s walk-on player, and he saw garbage-time minutes in four games this season. He had one offensive rebound during the 87-43 win over New Haven on Nov. 8.

Grade: N/A

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball set for another ranked matchup on the West Coast against USC
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 15 Penn State looks to get back in the win column as it prepares for its third straight match from the West Coast against No. 5 USC at 8 p.m. Saturday.

Coming off two straight losses in California, the Nittany Lions enter Saturday’s contest at 11-6. And with a 3-4 record on the road, it won’t be an easy feat as they prepare to take on their third straight ranked opponent.

If the blue and white want to break the two-game losing streak, it will have its work cut out.

The Trojans enter the matchup at 9-3, with a 6-2 home record. But, like the Nittany Lions, they endured a three-game losing streak until a sweep of Concordia on Thursday.

USC’s Dillon Klein ranks No. 2 in the NCAA in kills per set, averaging a massive 4.39. Klein rivals the blue and white’s Sean Harvey, who currently sits at No. 38 in the same category.

Klein currently tallies 201 kills on the year, but Harvey only trails slightly behind at 186. In fact, the two seem to be somewhat evenly matched ahead of Saturday’s match.

Klein won the MPSF/Molten Offensive Player of the Week honor in February, his seventh-career selection for the title and his second this season.

Similarly, Harvey received the EIVA Offensive Player of the Week honor twice this season, with his second also coming in the same week as Klein’s.

The pair alone should make for a high-intensity matchup on Saturday.

But Klein isn’t the only member of the USC roster that Penn State should keep an eye on.

Wesley Smith has been a hot topic for the Trojans this year. At No. 8 in the NCAA in blocks per set, the junior has 42 blocks on the year and averages 1.20 blocks per set.

That’s not to say Penn State doesn’t have defensive power of its own.

Owen Rose remains a standout for the blue and white this season. At No. 2 in the NCAA in blocks per set, the redshirt senior earned the EIVA Defensive Player of the Week title for the fifth time in his career.

Further, Penn State ranks No. 2 in the NCAA in blocks per set, whereas USC ranks tied at No. 11. With only 10 places between the two, it should be a defensive-heavy game.

But, offense might be what gets the better of the Nittany Lions.

USC enters the contest ranked No. 8 in hitting percentage within the NCAA with a .342 average. The blue and white, however, trail much farther behind at No. 25 with a .263.

But both teams seem to have their fair share of issues when it comes to serving.

Currently, the Trojans only have 58 service aces on the year, with Klein responsible for 14 of those.

The Nittany Lions, however, have 82 service aces on the season, with 19 of those going to Rose.

With both teams having a chance to get back in form on Saturday, it’s sure to be a ranked matchup worth watching.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State women’s soccer stars open NWSL season
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After illustrious careers in Happy Valley, it’s time for the professional leagues.

Between long-time NWSL veterans and former Penn Staters preparing for their first games, here’s a look at the former Nittany Lions who’ll be competing in the NWSL this season.

Orlando Pride - Kerry Abello, Cori Dyke

After graduating from Penn State in the last few years, Kerry Abello and Cori Dyke will be suiting up for the Orlando Pride again this season after an 11-8-7 season last year.

Abello only played in 20 contests last year with one goal after a hamstring injury kept her out for nearly two months. However, she’ll look to play a bigger role this year in her fifth year in the NWSL.

Meanwhile, Dyke saw only 12 games in Year 2, also finding the back of the net just one.

Kansas City Current - Elizabeth Ball, Ellie Bravo-Young, Penelope Hocking, Rocky Rodriguez, Katie Scott and Amelia White

Penn State’s historic collegiate program put half a dozen players on last year’s regular-season champions, the Kansas City Current.

Elizabeth Ball, Ellie Bravo-Young, Penelope Hocking, Rocky Rodriguez, Katie Scott and Amelia White will all suit up together following a 21-3-2 record last season.

Coming into her sixth year with the Current, Ball saw 15 starts on the defensive end last year. Also playing defense is Bravo-Young, who played 15 games in Year 2, scoring a goal and assisting on another.

Fellow rising third-year veteran Hocking made a big mark with Kansas City last year, scoring six goals with two assists in 13 games played.

After bouncing around with a few different teams after being selected second overall in the 2016 NWSL Draft, Rodriguez played her first year with the Current last season and made just seven starts.

One of the biggest newcomers in Kansas City is Scott, who is entering Year 2 after playing just one year collegiately at Penn State. She played in only two games last year but has plenty of time to improve at just 18 years old.

The last Nittany Lion with the Current is White, who enters her rookie year after 53 starts with the blue and white.

San Diego Wave - Jordan Fusco

While relatively new to Happy Valley after spending just her senior year at Penn State, Jordan Fusco enters Year 2 with the San Diego Wave.

Fusco saw limited time in her first year, making just seven appearances. However, after coming off a loan with Tampa Bay Sun FC of the USL Super League, Fusco will be ready for more action in her second season.

Racing Louisville - Ellie Jean

2018 graduate Ellie Jean enters just Year 5 in the NWSL after starting her career with a pair of Dutch clubs before rejoining the U.S. league in 2022.

Jean made 24 starts for Racing Louisville last year, scoring one goal from the defensive end.

North Carolina Courage - Payton Linnehan, Ally Schlegel

After graduating in 2023, Payton Linnehan spent one year with the Portland Thorns after being drafted 11th overall in the 2024 NWSL Draft before coming to the North Carolina Courage last season.

Linnehan earned a pair of assists in just two starts, helping the Courage to a 9-9-8 record.

Forward Ally Schlegel spent the last four years with the Chicago Stars before coming to the Courage this year. Last season, Schlegel started in 24 games and earned three goals and five assists.

Portland Thorns - Morgan Messner

Goalkeeper Morgan Messner was traded from Racing Louisville to the San Diego Wave FC before ultimately landing with the Portland Thorns last year.

Messner made just one appearance in the Concacaf W Champions Cup for the Thorns, where she recorded a clean slate at goalie.

Chicago Stars - Alyssa Naeher

Seventeen-year NWSL veteran Alyssa Naeher returns for Year 11 with the Chicago Stars after starting all 23 games last season.

The 2024 Best FIFA Women’s Goalkeeper recorded one clean slate last year alongside 75 saves with just 46 goals against.

Utah Royals - Kaleigh Riehl

After graduating in 2019, defender Kaleigh Riehl came to the Utah Royals in 2024 after spending the previous five years with four different squads.

Last year, Riehl made 10 starts as she helped her squad to a 6-13-7 record.

Washington Spirit - Kate Wiesner

As the only veteran to spend several years with one team, defender Kate Wiesner returns for Year 3 with the Washington Spirit.

After playing in a pair of matches with the U.S. national team, Wiesner started in 12 games last year as she led the Spirit to the NWSL championship game, before falling to Gotham FC 1-0.

MORE SOCCER COVERAGE

title: No. 18 Penn State women’s lacrosse dominates USC, remains unbeaten
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State remained on the West Coast for a matchup with USC, and while the weather in State College was below freezing, the weather in sunny Southern California was in the 80s.

The offensive momentum carried right into this matchup as the Nittany Lions walloped the Trojans, 19-8.

The return of Erika Ho to the lineup full-time was noticed early, as the Tewaarton Award Watch List member found the back of the net to kick things off.

Just three minutes later, Payton Wainman scored off a free-position goal to double the blue and white’s lead.

While all the momentum was seemingly on Penn State’s side, goalie Sydney Manning flipped the ball up and accidentally put it into her own net, shortening the Nittany Lions’ lead to 2-1.

The blue and white went on the man-up advantage and regained a two-goal lead on a goal from Emma Kelly. The Bethpage, New York, native picked up a free ball and immediately shot it on net, finding the back of the net.

USC responded, however, as Emma Bunting scored a little over two minutes later on a feed from Anna Regan. From there, it was the Wainman show to end the first quarter.

The Delray Beach, Florida, native scored two goals just one minute apart, both garnering assists from freshman star Rocquette Allen.

Wainman continued her run into the second quarter, notching her second free-position goal of the evening. With the blue and white on another man-up advantage, Anna Salerno scored, putting Penn State up 7-2.

The Trojans went on a 2-1 advantage over the next three minutes as Bunting and Reese Robinson got shots past Manning. In between those goals, Ho scored her second of the night to keep the Nittany Lions in a comfortable advantage.

The blue and white ended all potential momentum, flipping sides as a 4-0 run to end the half, putting the Nittany Lions up big. Wainman notched her fourth goal of the season before Kelly MacKinney scored her first.

While leading the team in assists, Delany Radin showcased her scoring ability, scoring the final two goals of the half. The second of the two goals from Radin was a behind-the-back shot, something the Nittany Lions have proved to have in their arsenal. Penn State entered halftime with a 12-4 lead.

The offense didn’t slow down to begin the half. Maddie Greco scored her eighth goal of the season just 30 seconds into the second half.

The Trojans responded with Robinson securing her second goal of the game, but the blue and white were right back on the attack. In a free-position shot opportunity, Radin took one step and ripped one past USC goalie Ellie Thomas.

Allen remained quiet on the goal-scoring front until she received a pass from behind the net and stepped right in front, sneaking her shot past Thomas. The Nittany Lions held a 15-5 lead halfway through the third quarter.

Penn State fired nonstop shots on Thomas, going 15-for-16 on shots, leading to a goalie pull with the Trojan netminder holding just a .063 save percentage.

Neither team found success for five straight minutes before Radin found a cutting MacKinney, who shot one into the net, extending the blue and white’s lead to 16-5. It was the first shot on new goaltender Annie Shields.

Two straight goals from Bunting forced a Penn State timeout with the Trojans starting to mount a comeback. Despite still holding a nine-point lead, coach Kayla Treanor wasn’t taking any chances, attempting to put a halt to momentum.

She did just that, as out of the timeout, MacKinney scored a free-position goal for her fourth of the night. The blue and white held a 17-7 lead heading into the fourth quarter.

The draw controls from Kelly continued to show up as the Nittany Lions started off with the ball. On a full rotation around the net, MacKinney found Ellie Wisch, who weaved her way into the circle and fired it past Shields.

Kelly earned another draw control and just moments later, Lizzie Anné scored her first of the night, putting her team up by 12.

With under five minutes to go, USC was making an all-out effort to restrict goals and mount the double-digit comeback. Regan secured her first of the night on a shot past Manning, pulling the score to 19-8.

While play went up and down the field, the scoring went to a minimum, as the Nittany Lions secured the 19-8 victory and a 2-0 West Coast trip.

Up next

Penn State will return to Happy Valley to host No. 22 Princeton at noon Sunday. It’ll be the third-ranked matchup of the season for the blue and white and another opportunity to build its resume.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: No. 3 Penn State women’s hockey hosts No. 6 UConn in NCAA Regional Final
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
After winning its fourth-straight AHA championship and setting a single-season record in wins with 32, No. 3 Penn State sets its sights on its fourth NCAA tournament run.

However, this time around, the Nittany Lions will host a regional game at Pegula Ice Arena for the first time in program history. UConn defeated Princeton 4-1 on Thursday to advance and face Penn State in the regional finals.

“It’s nice to be home in Pegula,” head coach Jeff Kampersal said. “Hopefully, next Saturday, people will come back early from spring break and cheer these women on. To be at home is awesome and they all work so hard for it. We don’t have to get on a bus and play wherever. That stuff makes a difference.”

The two teams met at the beginning of last season, splitting a weekend series. This contest marks the tenth overall between the programs, with the Huskies holding a 5-1-3 record over the blue and white.

The Huskies finished the season with a 17-6-1 conference record, 28-8-2 overall. They earned the second seed in the Hockey East tournament, ultimately defeating Northeastern in double overtime to claim the conference title.

Expect a goalie duel when the two squads go head-to-head. Tia Chan for UConn has been nothing short of impressive in her final collegiate season. The Hamilton, Ontario, native leads the Hockey East conference in goals against average with 1.53 and save percentage with a .951. All culminating in Chan being named a Patty Kazmaier award finalist.

At the other end of the ice, Katie DeSa has been a brick wall for Penn State. The Pawcatuck, Connecticut, native averaged a 1.32 goals against average, tied for first in the country. The senior also recorded the most shutouts in the nation with 11.

However, DeSa hasn’t faced as many shots as Chan faced throughout their respective conference tournaments. The Huskies’ netminder is coming off a 94-minute contest in which she recorded 57 saves, a number DeSa hasn’t seen all season.

The Penn State goalie will face a relentless offense that averaged 36.7 shots per game. Kyla Josifovic leads UConn in points with 40. The senior also scored the game-winning goal in the conference championship for the Huskies. Not too far behind her is Claire Murdoch with 30. The two forwards rounded out the top-five in points for the conference.

Opposite of them is a group of forwards consisting of five seniors who have contributed to one of the top-five offenses in the country. Leading the charge is the recently named top-three finalist for the Patty Kazmaier award, Tessa Janecke.

The Orangeville, Illinois, native leads the team in points with 45. However, she sits at second in goals behind Grace Outwater. Two other seniors – Maddy Christian and Katelyn Roberts – follow Janecke in goals, all contributing to a 4.3 goals per game for Penn State’s offense.

An area of the game that could pose the most crucial is the penalty department. The Huskies have only committed 86 penalties all season, while the Nittany Lions have totaled 148 penalties throughout the season.

However, that doesn’t mean Penn State’s penalty kill constantly suffered under the disadvantage. The Nittany Lions recorded eight shorthanded goals over the season, averaging an 84.8 penalty kill percentage. If faced with discipline problems, the unit will face a top-15 power-play in UConn. The Huskies posted a 21.4 power-play percentage that ranked them 14th in the nation.

The stage has been set for Penn State’s first-ever home NCAA regional game. For the Nittany Lions, this marks the beginning of an accomplishment worked towards throughout the season.

“(The team) came together as a group before the season and went over our goals this year,” Katie DeSa said. “I have it written on my fridge, I look at it every day, and it’s to host regionals and to make a Frozen Four appearance. To be able to do that at home is just a really awesome feeling.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: No. 19 Penn State women's gymnastics faces No. 18 UNC, Nebraska on the road to end regular season
author: Caroline Riley | The Daily Collegian
description:
The No. 19 Nittany Lions will hit the road to face No. 18 UNC and Nebraska in Chapel Hill for their last regular-season meet of the season on Friday, March 13th, at 7 p.m.

Coming off its 14th straight 196.000+ score, Penn State will look to extend that streak one last time before the postseason.

The blue and white currently have an NQS of 196.478, which could be improved with a strong away score this weekend. In order to best last week’s 196.550, Penn State will need a repeat of some key performances.

Sophomore Dani Latronica has been a star for Penn State this season. She consistently puts up 9.900s on bars and beam, while contributing on vault as well. Other Nittany Lions who have shown that coveted 9.900+ potential include freshman Ava Cash and senior Ava Piedrahita.

Penn State is a strong team on bars and beam, but needs to improve on floor exercise to earn a higher overall team score. Cleaning not only the tumbling passes but also the takeoff and air positions in the leap series will be important for getting every tenth.

Beyond the floor, Penn State has plenty of room for improvement in the vault as well. The Nittany Lions have two 10.0 start-value vaults to anchor their lineup, but Allison Kaempfer has not been the most consistent with her Yurchenko 1.5.

The lineup starts with a string of Yurchenko fulls, starting with a 9.950. Penn State needs to focus not only on clean landings but also on minimizing in-air deductions to boost its vault score.

Facing the team directly in front of them in the national rankings, the Nittany Lions will need postseason energy and clean gymnastics to take home an upset win this weekend.

North Carolina

No. 18 University of North Carolina has even more riding on this meet than just a strong end to the season, as the Tar Heels will be celebrating their Senior Night. North Carolina is hosting with momentum. The Tar Heels are coming off a big win against No. 13 Minnesota, where they posted their fourth-best score in program history with a 197.200.

Repeating a 197.000+ performance again this weekend will pose a real challenge to Penn State and Nebraska. The Nittany Lions have posted two scores higher than that mark this season, so they will need that same big performance to beat the Tar Heels on their home turf.

North Carolina has a lot of star power, including freshman all-around threat Sydney Seabrooks. She has posted four 9.950s on the floor this year, which puts her in a tie for 6th in the NCAA, and she is coming fresh off an all-around win against Minnesota with a 39.450.

To challenge Latronica on bars, the Tar Heels can look to senior Gwen Fink, who has recorded a career-high 9.950 this season. Coming off such a big upset, North Carolina could be peaking at the right time and look to down Penn State and Nebraska one last time before the postseason.

Nebraska

Nebraska is the only unranked competitor in Friday’s matchup. Going up against the No. 18 and No. 19 teams in the country is not an easy feat. The Huskers are coming off a road victory over Texas Women’s University, where they earned a 195.600.

Earlier this season, Penn State beat Nebraska 197.225-195.200, so redemption could be in order for the Huskers. Nebraska will look to its junior star, Whitney Jencks. Last week, she won the all-around with a 39.350, which included a career-high 9.925 on the bars.

With each Big Ten team looking to better its NQS and put up a strong showing to end the regular season, Friday’s matchup is poised to be a glimpse into postseason gymnastics.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field prepares for NCAA championship
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a very successful regular season and Big Ten championship, Penn State is on to the final challenge.

Twelve Nittany Lions will compete in the NCAA championship on Friday and Saturday at the University of Arkansas after earning the top 16 times, distances and scores in their events.

Here’s a look at how Penn State’s athletes stack up to the rest of the field at this year’s championship event.

Women’s pentathlon

Senior Maddie Pitts has one final go-around in her collegiate indoor campaign, entering this weekend with the No. 5 score in the country.

Pitts set the school record at the Big Ten championship in February with a mark of 4,384 points, where she earned personal-bests in the high jump and long jump.

She’ll race all five events in the pentathlon on Friday, beginning with the 60-meter hurdles at 12:15 p.m., followed by the high jump, shot put, long jump and 800-meters at 6 p.m.

Of the four opponents ahead of her, Pitts faced Illinois’ Megan Humphries and JaiCieonna Gero-Holt at the conference championship, who finished first and second, respectively. They enter the national meet at first and fourth — ahead of Pitts by 75 and 16 points, respectively — along with Cincinnati’s Juliette Laracuente-Huebner, who’s 53 points ahead, and Kansas State’s Vanessa Mercera, who’s 45 points ahead.

Men’s 60m

While only in his sophomore season, Ajani Dwyer has made a name for himself as he prepares for his second indoor NCAA championship.

Dwyer will compete in the men’s 60-meter, where he holds the seventh-best time in the country. He’ll compete in the semifinals at 6:45 p.m. on Friday, followed by the finals at 5:40 p.m. on Saturday.

The Sewell, New Jersey, native ran a school record 6.52 seconds at the Sykes & Sabock Challenge in early February, which is the third-best time in his heat on Friday.

He’ll have to finish top-four in his heat — or the next-best two runners — to qualify for the finals, competing against Big Ten champion Kalen Walker and a pair of sub-6.50 SEC runners.

Men’s 400m

After falling just 0.11 seconds shy of a Big Ten title, Jake Palermo will have a chance to redeem himself in the men' s 400-meter at 7:10 p.m. Friday and 6 p.m. on Saturday.

Palermo enters the race with the fifth-best time in the country, a school record he set at the Tyson Invitational — the same track he’ll race on this weekend — with a time of 45.33 seconds.

The sophomore sprinter will race in the second heat on Friday, and will need to run one of the eight fastest times to book his spot in Saturday’s finals, racing against South Carolina’s Josiah Wrice, Ohio State’s Edidiong Udo, and Arizona State’s Jayden Davis.

Women’s 800m

Senior Hayley Kitching and freshman Boh Ritchie will compete in the women’s 800-meter at 7:25 p.m. on Friday and 6:10 p.m. on Saturday for the finals.

Kitching enters the race with the second-best time in the country after winning the Big Ten championship last month. She broke the school record in 1:59.22 at the Husky Classic in February, and is one of five athletes who have run sub-2 minutes at the championship race.

Ritchie finished fourth at the conference meet last month, just missing out on a medal, but is back in the second heat as she’ll race Clemson’s Gladys Chepngetich, Colorado’s Avery Pearson and others for a chance at a finals appearance on Saturday.

Men’s 800m

The Nittany Lions have 25% of the entries in the men’s 800m, as Handal Roban, Allon Clay, Yukichi Ishii and Niko Schultz will all compete in the prelims at 7:35 p.m. Friday for a chance at the finals at 6:20 p.m. on Saturday.

Roban enters the race with the best time in the nation, running a 1:44.73 earlier this year. The Big Ten champion is the fastest in his heat, and will help push Schultz and Ishii to qualify for the finals despite their 12th and 16th-seeded times.

Meanwhile, Clay is the only Penn Stater in Heat 2, but has the No. 3 time in the country and the second-best time in his heat. His biggest competitor will be Georgetown’s Tinoda Matsatsa, who has the second-best time in the country.

Men’s 200m

Dwyer is the only Nittany Lion to compete individually in two events in this year’s NCAA championship, as he’ll also hit the track for the men’s 200-meter at 9 p.m. on Friday.

The sophomore sensation enters the race with the ninth-best time in the country, but arguably has the toughest path to Saturday’s 6:40 p.m. finals as he’s racing Arkansas’ Jelani Watkins and USC’s Garrett Kaalund — the two best athletes in the event.

Men’s DMR

Clay, Roban and Schultz have another chance to race this weekend in the men’s distance medley relay, where they’ll join Nick Sloff with the 10th-best time in the country.

Competing at 9:30 p.m. on Friday, the Nittany Lions will be challenged by nine teams ahead of them, including four Big Ten squads that raced at the conference championship.

This year’s squad shattered the school record at the Philly Metro earlier this year, running a time of 9:18.58.

Men’s shot put

Ryan Henry and Dylan Lambrecht are the only Nittany Lions who’ll wait until Saturday before they first see any competition, competing in the men’s shot put at 2 p.m.

Lambrecht is the first to throw, followed by Henry being the 11th. However, it’s Henry who holds the better distance this season with a throw of 19.94 meters — the third-best in school history — followed by Lambrecht’s 16th seed of 19.57 meters, the fifth-best.

Outside of Oregon’s Kobe Lawrence and Nebraska’s Cade Moran at second and sixth in the country, who Henry upset for the Big Ten title last month, Ole Miss’s Tarik Robinson-O’Hagan will be the biggest challenge, over a meter ahead of any other athlete with a season-best 21.18 meters.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Report: Former Penn State S Jaquan Brisker signs with Pittsburgh Steelers
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Joey Porter Jr. and Jaquan Brisker are reuniting in the big leagues.

Brisker is reportedly signing with the Pittsburgh Steelers on a one-year, $5.5 million contract, per Ian Rapoport.

The former Nittany Lions safety spent the first four years of his NFL career with the Chicago Bears, who selected Brisker in the second round of the 2022 NFL Draft.

Brisker tallied 21 pass deflections, seven sacks and four interceptions with Chicago.

Porter and Brisker shared the defensive backfield at Penn State from 2019-21, and they’ll now play together once again in Pittsburgh.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball moves Friday's start time up against Iowa
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State will begin Big Ten play even sooner after the start time for its series opener against Iowa was moved up.

The Nittany Lions announced Friday's first pitch is now scheduled at 3:30 p.m. instead of the previous 5:30 p.m. start.

🚨Game Time Update🚨Friday's game against Iowa has been moved up to a 3:30 p.m. start at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park➡️ https://t.co/k4kLqACW7D#WeAre pic.twitter.com/pYsWF2GNzG— Penn State Baseball (@PennStateBASE) March 12, 2026

Friday's contest is the first of a three-game series against the Hawkeyes. The other start times are slated at 2 p.m. Saturday and at 1 p.m. Sunday.

Penn State takes a five-game losing streak and a 4-11 record into the series and looks to record its first series win of the campaign.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Column | Penn State women’s basketball’s ‘must-keep’ players amidst coaching change
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coming off a historically poor season, a lot of change is in store following the firing of former coach Carolyn Kieger.

With an additional 15-day transfer portal window in addition to the regular period, a lot of Lady Lions are expected to find a new home. However, here are a few must-keep players that the new coach and administration should do everything in their power to keep in Happy Valley.

Kiyomi McMiller

This one is the most obvious, as sophomore Kiyomi McMiller kept the Lady Lions alive down the final stretch of the season.

McMiller averaged the 12th-most points in the country this year, as she recorded six straight 30-point games to end the season.

While the Silver Spring, Maryland, native was dominant with the ball in her hand, she also found success in other aspects on the court. McMiller averaged five rebounds, 4.5 assists, and 1.8 steals on the year as she recorded three double-doubles.

With her best basketball coming in the final weeks of the season, McMiller’s success could carry over into next season and be paired with several other talented returners and incoming freshman Chikae Desdunes.

Gracie Merkle

After becoming one of the best centers in Lady Lions history, Gracie Merkle has a chance to cement herself as the greatest in her senior year.

Merkle led the nation in field goal percentage her redshirt junior year, averaging 72.5% with 19.2 points per game.

Through the first half of the season, Merkle earned easy baskets in the paint with her 6-foot-6 frame.

As the season progressed, she was double and triple-teamed, limiting her field goal scoring but sending her to the free throw line more. Merkle ended the season with 68 free throws, tied for the most on the team.

Tèa Clèante

Despite only one year in Happy Valley, freshman Tea Clèante made a big mark with the Lady Lions.

The Dunkirk, France, native started in 24 of Penn State’s 29 games this year, averaging 8.7 points and 2.9 assists per game.

While her stats weren’t breathtaking, Clèante’s defensive pressure was among the best on the team. After participating in the Eurobasket qualifiers over the next several months, Clèante would come back to Penn State with even more experience heading into Year 2.

Nyla McFadden

Freshman Nyla McFadden was another young star who made big contributions to the blue and white.

Down the back half of the season, McFadden saw the starting lineup eight times and tallied six double-digit scoring performances on the season.

As the season progressed, so did McFadden’s skills on the court, which could take another big leap in Year 2.

Shayla Smith

Freshman Shayla Smith didn’t see a lot of action during her freshman year, averaging just 9.9 minutes through 20 games. However, she did have high expectations coming to Penn State, which could continue to grow if she stuck around another year.

Smith came to Happy Valley as one of Philadelphia’s best high school basketball players of all time, totalling 2,666 points in four years.

While she was injured during the first part of her freshman year, Smith never got the minutes many were expecting, with her best moments in a 12-point performance against Arizona State and 13 points against South Carolina.

MORE BASKETBALL CONTENT

title: Penn State men’s volleyball unable to defeat Pepperdine, falls in four sets
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions took to the court from Malibu, California, looking to get back in the win column after their first loss in eight games against Long Beach State last week.

Ultimately, No. 15 Penn State fell short 3-1 in Wednesday’s ranked matchup against No. 6 Pepperdine.

The blue and white seemed to struggle in the opening moments of the first set. After some brief back-and-forth, Pepperdine pulled away and scored three straight points, putting it up 10-5.

Penn State was forced to take an early timeout and regroup.

While Sean Harvey was able to deliver a kill to resume play, the Waves were too hot to slow down.

After getting a few quick points, the blue and white allowed four straight points from Pepperdine, two of which were back-to-back service aces from the Waves’ Jose Gomez.

Gaige Gabriel managed a few big kills, along with teammate Owen Rose, but the Pepperdine squad was off and running.

The Waves took set one 25-16, with nearly half of Penn State's points coming from serving and attack errors by Pepperdine.

Opening the second set, the blue and white took the first two points with a service error from the Waves and a block assist from Matthew Luoma and Rose.

But the brief lead was quickly shut down as Pepperdine came back with five straight points, three of which were service aces from Cole Hartke. The Nittany Lions were forced to take yet another early timeout down 4-7.

While Luoma was able to get two kills following the break, Pepperdine continued to pull away with it.

Despite allowing three straight points to the Waves, a challenged call from the Nittany Lions got them right back in it. After scoring two straight points, the Pepperdine lead closed to 12-8.

Kills from Harvey and Luoma helped the blue and white to close the lead within three at 13-10, and it seemed that service and attack errors from the Waves were keeping the Nittany Lions in the game.

For the next several points, Penn State kept the score within three, with every opposing point being met with a point of its own. But after challenging a Penn State point, Pepperdine was able to reverse the call and put its lead at 18-13.

After a miscommunication between Tristan Hassell and Rose, the Nittany Lions called yet another timeout down 22-14.

Harvey logged another kill, as did Hassell, but it just wasn’t enough to stop the Waves. The second set went to Pepperdine 25-16.

The third set opened with back-to-back points from the Waves, taking an early lead. Scoring went back and forth, and the score stayed tight on both sides.

Harvey tallied his first ace of the match in the early moments of the set, making it 5-4. But again, Pepperdine responded immediately.

A long rally saw another much-needed point from the blue and white. After a dig from libero Ryan Merk and a huge block assist from Hassell and Rose, the blue and white closed the lead to one.

A series of kills from Gabriel and Harvey, as well as a block assist from Jaxon Herr and Rose, gave Penn State three straight points, putting it up 10-9 and giving it a chance of hope.

Even though Pepperdine got right back in it with back-to-back points, the blue and white responded in the same manner, tying it up at 12-12.

Scoring continued to go back and forth on both sides, keeping the game tied. After a kill and service ace from Hassell, followed by a kill from Merk, Pepperdine was forced to take a timeout as it trailed 17-15.

After some more back-and-forth, Pepperdine scored three straight to tie things again at 19-19, forcing a Penn State timeout.

Gabriel put up a necessary kill and back-to-back aces to increase the blue and white’s lead 22-19, forcing a Pepperdine timeout in the late moments of the set.

After the timeout, Harvey put up a massive kill to force set point at 24-22. A kill from Hassell ended the set 25-22, forcing a fourth set.

It seemed the Nittany Lions had settled in. The fourth set opened with four straight from the blue and white, making it 4-0.

Pepperdine wasn’t going to make it easy, though. It eventually went on to score nine straight, making it 10-5.

From there, it was smooth sailing for the Pepperdine squad. Unable to recover from the lead, the Nittany Lions fell in the fourth and final set of the match 25-16, handing them their second straight loss.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions will remain in California as they face No. 5 USC from the Galen Center at 8 p.m. Saturday.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball survives Oregon State, earns first win of West Coast trip
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State traveled cross-country for its last game before beginning Big Ten play and faced Oregon State on Wednesday night. While the Nittany Lions fell out of the top 25 rankings after going 2-2 at the Hokie Invite last weekend, they had an opportunity to get back on track against the 10-17 Beavers.

After losing two straight games, Penn State bounced back behind solid pitching and late runs in its 3-1 win over Oregon State.

Though the blue and white’s offense has been rolling throughout the season, right-hander Kimberlee Aleman prevented the Nittany Lions from plating any runs in the first four innings. Leadoff third baseman Allison Oneacre knocked a single to shortstop Camrynn Gordon.

A few batters later, catcher Macy Chamberlin launched a two-out double into left field and Penn State quickly had two runners in scoring position. However, first baseman Gabby Gradishar ended the threat with a groundout to third baseman Paige Bambarger.

Right-hander Abigail Britton also ran into some traffic on the basepaths in the opening frame, but prevented any runs from scoring. Morgan Howey raked a two-out double to left center, but Britton retired the next Beaver batter on an infield pop-up to shortstop Kalli Karwowski.

Aleman shut down the Nittany Lions in order and the Beavers took the early lead in the bottom half of the second. Mya Harrison started the scoring by drawing a long one-out walk and though Britton struck out Madison Weir, Oregon State put together some two-out magic.

Harrison stole second and Bambarger worked a long walk, which kept the inning alive. Gordon loaded the bases with an infield single to Oneacre at third. Jada Lewis delivered in a similar way to Gordon’s hit and drove in a run off an infield single to third.

However, Britton stopped the bleeding and Penn State was down just one run entering the third. Despite the blue and white going down in order once again, right-hander Mackenzie Duncan was brought into the game and delivered a pivotal shutout inning.

There were no base runners for either squad in the fourth, but the Nittany Lions’ bats tied the game in the fifth. Left fielder Breanna Hanik recorded the first hit for Penn State since the opening frame and crushed a lead-off double deep into center.

Right fielder Cara Bohner tapped a one-out infield single back to the mound and Hanik turned on the jets, advancing to third. Second baseman Michela Barbanente grounded out to third, but plated Hanik. With Bohner in scoring position, Oneacre couldn’t capitalize and flew out to center field.

Coach Clarisa Crowell went back to the bullpen with the game tied at one and brought in left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris. The freshman came through and retired the side in order.

The blue and white entered the late innings with a chance at taking the lead for the first time. In the sixth, the Nittany Lions continued the offensive momentum and scored two more runs.

Center fielder Natalie Lieto poked a lead off single to left and designated player Brooke Klosowicz followed with a hard-hit single up the middle. Lieto and Klosowicz also reached second and third off the throw.

Right-hander Amelia Streuber relieved Aleman, but couldn’t stop the damage. Klosowicz was out at third off Chamberlin’s ground ball to shortstop, but Frankee Flesher's pitch ran for Chamberlin and stole second.

With runners in scoring position, Gradishar came through and knocked an RBI single into left to give Penn State its first lead of the night. Hanik lifted a routine fly ball to left fielder Lewis, but she dropped the ball, and another run was plated.

Bohner ended the frame in a double play lineout to shortstop, but the blue and white took a 3-1 lead late. Bar an error by Oneacre at third, which allowed the lead off to reach safely, Kellepouris provided another scoreless inning.

Kellepouris sealed the victory in the seventh and the Nittany Lions snapped their two-game winning streak and improved to 19-6 with the victory.

Up Next

Penn State stays on the West Coast and begins Big Ten play with a three-game series against No. 18 Oregon. The series opener is slated for 9 p.m. Friday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 10 Penn State earns dominant win over Minnesota, advances in Big Ten Tournament
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
One week ago, No. 10 Penn State was considered a lock to make the NCAA Tournament. But after suffering a series sweep at the hands of then-No. 11 Wisconsin that dropped them to 2-6-2 over their last 10 games, the Nittany Lions’ bid was no longer secure.

Those results gave far greater importance to Wednesday’s Big Ten Tournament opener between No. 3 seed Penn State and No. 6 seed Minnesota. The Golden Gophers, who went just 11-21-3 this season, entered Pegula Ice Arena in a win-or-go-home scenario, while the Nittany Lions looked to bolster their tournament resume.

Penn State used a dominant second period to earn a decisive 6-2 victory and move on to the second round of the Big Ten Tournament.

Jackson Smith thought he had opened the scoring when, less than three minutes into play, his backhand shot grazed Luca Di Pasquo’s pad and looked primed to hit the net before it pinged off the crossbar. But a minute later, Smith’s teammates had his back.

Matt DiMarsico used his body to keep the puck inside the offensive zone, which allowed JJ Wiebusch to corral the loose puck and center a feed to Charlie Cerrato. In just his second game back from injury, the sophomore forward ripped a wrister across his body and past Di Pasquo to give Penn State an early 1-0 lead.

Following a disappointing series in which it allowed 12 goals to the Badgers, Guy Gadowsky’s squad needed a bounce-back defensive performance. And respond it did.

It took Minnesota nearly 10 minutes to record its first shot on goal, a routine pad save by goaltender Josh Fleming. So it was only right that a defenseman get rewarded with the game’s second goal.

Gavin McKenna waited patiently atop the zone for teammates to join the rush before he fed a streaking Mac Gadowsky. The Army transfer, who tallied 16 goals last season, one-timed a slap shot past Di Pasquo for his first goal as a Nittany Lion.

Minnesota’s power play threatened late, but Fleming stood tall as Penn State entered the first intermission ahead 2-0 in a game it had to have.

Penn State’s domination continued into the second frame.

Amid a pressure-filled shift, Lev Katzin scooped a loose puck and ripped it toward an open net, but he was robbed by Di Pasquo’s outstretched glove. The freshman forward didn’t score, but his line’s impactful shift tilted the ice, which led to a pair of Penn State goals.

The first tally was sparked by a Jarod Crespo feed to DiMarsico, who deked through two defenders before he beat Di Pasquo five-hole to make it 3-0. It marked DiMarsico’s second point of the contest and 100th of his career.

Crespo recorded another primary assist less than a minute later, this one a slap shot that caromed off Di Pasquo’s glove and onto Reese Laubach’s awaiting stick. The junior forward snapped it home to extend Penn State’s lead to 4-0.

Minnesota pulled one back late in the second period when Javon Moore batted Smith’s pass onto his stick and threw a shot on goal that trickled under Fleming’s pad. It was a rare blunder for the Nittany Lions in an otherwise impressive two periods.

Penn State switched from an aggressive offensive approach to a more measured defensive strategy in the final period. The Golden Gophers turned their increased puck possession into several point-blank chances, but Fleming’s outstretched glove denied them all.

And minutes later, Aiden Fink put the dagger in Minnesota’s season. After his back-door feed went by his intended skater, the Nashville Predators prospect regained possession before sniping one top tight to give Penn State a four-goal cushion at the mid-way point of the third period.

McKenna recorded the primary assist on Fink’s goal. It marked his 36th helper and 50th point of the season.

Jacob Rombach scored through traffic to make it 5-2. But the defenseman’s first goal of the season wasn’t enough to spark a comeback.

Cade Christensen added an empty-net goal to seal a dominant home win for Penn State.

Up Next

Penn State will travel to Ann Arbor on Saturday to face No. 2 seed Michigan in the Big Ten Tournament semifinals. The game will be broadcast on Big Ten Network. The start time hasn’t been announced.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Column | Penn State men’s basketball’s 5 best moments in historically bad season
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Well, just like that, it’s time to wrap up the Nittany Lions’ 2025-26 season, and put the bow, as best possible, on a 12-20 finish where Penn State finished bottom of the Big Ten just as predicted.

The blue and white posted its worst season during Mike Rhoades' tenure, and in five years, since the 2020-21 season under Jim Ferry, which saw 11 wins.

But you know what? While the Nittany Lions did record their worst defensive season in the program’s 130-year history, I’m not going into the offseason with a bitter outlook. Optimism and a glass-half-full mentality have always suited me, and I feel they align perfectly with Penn State, the youngest unit in the Big Ten, so that’s the lens we’re going to use here, folks.

While some might jump to the negatives, it's time to look at the flip side – the five best moments of Penn State's season.

Almost beating Michigan State

Penn State’s Big Ten opener, an 8-2 record on the season, still in the late months of 2025, things actually seemed optimistic for the team and its prospects on the season in defying expectations.

That conference opener only solidified that optimism, while only brief, a 76-72 loss to an AP top-ten opponent, Michigan State, seemed to provide reason to believe that some result would come for Rhoades’ unit. When the Spartans’ legendary coach, Tom Izzo, or Mr. March, depending on how you know him, took to the Bryce Jordan Center media room with bombastic praise for the blue and white.

“This team is going to beat some people,” Izzo said. “This team is going to have some ups and downs because that's what young teams do. But boy, if (Rhoades) can hold this team together, I think this team's got a chance. They seem to get along well together. They pull from one another. I was impressed. And I'm not just saying that to make everybody feel better.”

It’s worth reading the rest of Izzo’s complementary remarks on Penn State, and for a season that had almost no good parts, this feels like a moment worth at least recalling back to in the sake of boosting morale.

Recapping the rest of a loss feels moot, but keeping it close and earning Izzo's admiration feels special – even though his prediction really didn’t come true, no one was beaten.

Give him the keys

Well, as soon as Penn State resumed its Big Ten schedule, the expected struggles persisted with eight straight conference losses after the first pair. However, on Feb. 1, Kayden Mingo, a 6-foot-3 guard from Farmingdale, New York, a freshman and Penn State’s top recruit of all time, scored a buzzer-beating highlight spin move to secure the Nittany Lions' first Big Ten victory.

Mingo lived up to most hype this season, with numbers that put him in elite company and really just show the do-it-all player he can be. Going into numbers, stretches or coaches’ commentary pales in comparison to this play against Minnesota; this was the paramount moment of the rookie’s breakout campaign.

Flirting with Dylan Mingo

Keeping it in the family with Kayden’s little brother, Dylan, we’ve got a slightly different moment that I’ve got to vouch for that is a little special. Penn State was in high running for recruiting the five-star combo guard to play with his brother, until he ultimately decided to head to UNC on First Take.

But Penn State was there, the blue and white were in the top four with massive programs in the Tarheels, Baylor and Washington and got that brief moment on ESPN – for a team that doesn’t get that recognition, or even that consideration from prospects, it’s crucial to at least acknowledge a moment that goes against the grain of historical narratives, no matter how it happened.

Reed-sanity run

The lone senior on Penn State’s incredibly youthful squad, Josh Reed’s services were leaned on and necessary in this past season. The Atlanta native averaged a mere 4.8 points per game in his last season with Cincinnati, but over doubled that figure in his only Happy Valley season.

The greatest moment of it all? A 13-game stretch where Reed reached double-digit scoring and even broke his career high twice, eventually setting the bar at 25 points. The wing was playing with an unprecedented level of personal confidence, and it rubbed off on his teammates, creating a memorable blip in Penn State history.

Reed did eventually falter on Senior Night, honoring him, with the streak snapped that night in a crushing 32-point loss to Ohio State.

Pat Kraft knows hoops exist?

Well, that Senior Night, possibly the lowest point this season, is where we’ll finish our journey, funny enough, with positive moments.

The basketball program at Penn State has long been a laughingstock, both men’s and women’s, and has been seen as the main sport neglected by the administration when it comes to allocating needed resources. But on that blowout to the Buckeyes, Athletic Director Pat Kraft was there in attendance for all 40 minutes. At least we can say he knows Penn State basketball exists, and now sees how bad it really is; maybe those resources can finally come. After all, they rose in KenPom over 60 spots – potential.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling holds 7 top seeds for NCAA Championships
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Following seven Penn Stater wrestlers winning Big Ten titles, the NCAA seeds have been released for the national tournament beginning on March 19.

The Nittany Lions will have seven wrestlers at No. 1 seeds.

Luke Lilledahl, Shayne Van Ness, PJ Duke, Mitchell Mesenbrink, Levi Haines, Rocco Welsh and Josh Barr all earned top spots in their respective weight classes. All seven of those wrestlers became 2026 Big Ten Champions.

At 133 pounds, Marcus Blaze earned the No. 3 seed after finishing second at the Big Ten Championships. Blaze sits behind Oklahoma State’s Jax Forrest and Ohio State’s Ben Davino in the weight class. If both wrestlers win out, it’ll be a rubber match between Davino and Blaze in the semifinals.

At 141 pounds, Braeden Davis sits at the No. 14 seed. Davis finished seventh at the Big Tens and will face Utah Valley's Haiden Drury in the opening round.

Cole Mirasola at heavyweight is the No. 9 seed. Mirasola ended the Big Ten Championships with a fifth-place finish.

As a team, Penn State will attempt to go for a fifth straight national title beginning on Thursday, March 19, in Cleveland at Rocket Arena.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball opens Big Ten play at home against Iowa
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After an unsuccessful 0-5 road trip, Penn State returns home and begins Big Ten play against Iowa. The three-game series is slated at 5:30 p.m. Friday, 2 p.m. Saturday, with the series finale at 1 p.m. Sunday. All games will be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

The Nittany Lions are in a tough position entering the series on a five-game losing streak and a 4-11 overall record. Many upset losses in favorable contests against nonconference opponents has forced the blue and white to get its season back on track facing tough conference play.

Meanwhile, the Hawkeyes bring a two-game winning streak and a solid 11-5 record into Happy Valley, so Penn State recording its first series win of the campaign will be a tall task.

Key trends

Iowa possesses the best team batting average in the Big Ten at .344 and has scored the third-most runs at 145. Despite the Hawkeyes only totaling 10 home runs, they thrive with small ball as their 192 hits rank first in the conference.

The Nittany Lions pitchers need to limit runs this weekend if they want any chance of winning the series. It’s been a rocky year on the mound for Penn State, ranking last in the conference in team-ERA at 8.30 and surrendering the most hits (157) and most runs (132).

Most of the blue and white’s losses have been blowouts, providing the offense with little chance at keeping the Nittany Lions close in games. Right-hander Colin Fitzgerald was expected to be the anchor in the pitching staff, but the Maine transfer has struggled in three of his four starts.

The redshirt senior holds a 6.75 ERA and allowed 22 hits, nine walks and 12 earned runs over 16 innings. However, Fitzgerald delivered a solid performance in his second start in Penn State’s 5-0 win over Richmond, throwing a scoreless six innings and totaling eight strikeouts.

If the Lititz, Pennsylvania, native can channel some of that performance against Iowa, the Nittany Lions will be in good shape. Fitzgerald having a quality outing against a powerful lineup is also good for his confidence for the rest of the season.

Two other pitchers who might start games this weekend are right-handers Ben Hudson and Isaiah Shayter. Hudson provided a quality outing in Penn State’s 9-3 win over Grand Canyon, but his three starts since have been rocky.

The West Virginia transfer holds a 7.36 ERA, while surrendering 18 hits and 15 earned runs. Shayter’s 14.34 ERA was ballooned after his last start in the blue and white’s 14-4 loss against Texas Tech.

The freshman went just 1.2 innings and allowed five earned runs off five hits and five walks. So, the Elizabeth Town, Pennsylvania, native must last longer if he gets a start against Iowa.

Players to watch

The Hawkeyes’ lineup is stacked throughout, but there are some notable hitters the Nittany Lions must contain. Mitch Wood is one of the best all-around batters in the Big Ten.

The senior possesses a .414 average, 1.330 OPS, 12 hits and 15 RBI. Iowa doesn’t hit home runs often, but he leads the team in the category with three.

Gable Mitchell spearheads the offense with a team-best .484 average, 31 hits, 17 RBI and 12 walks.

While Penn State’s offense ranks in the bottom three for many key offensive stats in the Big Ten, Bryce Molinaro was a bright spot in its two-game series against Georgetown.

The redshirt junior homered in both games and brought his home run total to four. Michael Anderson continued his hot start and smashed his team-best seventh home run in Wednesday’s 5-4 win against the Hoyas.

Iowa’s pitching staff enters the series with a 5.18 team ERA, allowing 133 hits and 80 earned runs. The Nittany Lions will likely face the Hawkeyes’ ace, right-hander Maddux Frese.

The junior owns a 2.50 ERA, totaling 13 strikeouts, while also surrendering just 15 hits, 3 walks and five earned runs over 18 innings.

Penn State’s pitching and offense must click together to record its first series win this weekend.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball drops second game against Georgetown, going winless in series
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State baseball faced off against Georgetown in Washington, D.C., in its final game of a two-series matchup. Ultimately, Penn State would lose to Georgetown 5-4.

Penn State started out the day in the batter's box. Outfielder Cohl Mercado fouled out to start the order. Infielder Michael Anderson flew out, followed by infielder Spencer Barnett, who went down swinging. A strong start for Georgetown right-handed pitcher JT Rabb.

Nittany Lions freshman right-handed pitcher Ethan Bauerschmidt started the game on the mound in the bottom of the first. The Hoyas started off hot with a triple down the right field line by outfielder Ashtin Gilio.

Outfielder Travis Ilitch continued with a single. Infielder and pitcher Connor Peek flew out to center field, but that allowed Gilio to make it home for the game's first run. Bauerschmidt threw one out, and catcher Connor Price grounded out to end the inaugural inning.

Penn State fired back quickly, coming from a Bryce Molinaro home run to right-center field. Though the hot start would fizzle with two groundouts, Preston Yaucher went down swinging. Raab let up a double from Jayden Davis and walked Joey DeMucci, who went on to steal second base.

The second stint on defense for the blue and white was a fast one. Bauerschmidt struck out the first two Hoyas batters looking. Then, infielder AJ Solomon popped one up to shortstop to end the second inning. The score was tied 1-1 going into the third.

Back to the top of the order for the Nittany Lions, Mercado flew out to start the hitting. Anderson and Barnett hit back-to-back homers to center and right-center, respectively. Molinaro grounded out after his homerun in the last inning and Jack Porter struck out swinging. Penn State led 3-1, only scoring by going the distance.

Gilio flew out on his second at-bat. Ilitch singled through the right side to get back on the positive for Georgetown. Bauerschmidt hit Peek with a pitch to advance the runners. Jeremy Sheffield moved the runners again after an error from shortstop Yaucher.

Christian Hamilton reached on a fielder's choice, advancing the runners and allowing Ilitch to score unearned. Hamilton was then caught stealing to end the inning. Penn State still led 3-2 after an inning of mishaps.

The Hoyas started the 4th with a pitching change; they swapped Rabb for freshman right-handed pitcher Ashton Arroyo. Davis got to first with a bunt, followed by Jesse Jaconski getting hit by a pitch. A Yaucher single led to a Davis score and Jaconski getting thrown out when he tried to advance to third.

Georgetown made another pitching change from Arroyo to left-handed pitcher Andrew Jergins, now their third of the game. Yaucher got caught stealing second to end Penn State's at-bats.

Price was walked, then got to second with the help of Dylan Larkins bunt, a sacrifice play. Dante Pozzi hit a homer to left field for a two-run play. After that, the Hoyas had two groundouts to end the 4th. The score stood tied up 4-4 going into the 5th inning.

Mercado struck out swinging and Anderson got the first hit for the Nittany Lions in the inning. Barnett then grounded a ball into a double play. A quick inning of batting for Penn State.

A pitching change got Bauerschmidt off the mound and gave right-handed pitcher Braden Leek his chance. Coach Mike Gambino also made changes to catcher and first base; he moved Avery Smith to catcher and DeMucci to first.

Leed began his pitching with a walk for Ilitch, who got caught stealing for an out. Peek lined out, and Sheffield swung out to end a fast offensive series for Georgetown.

The Hoyas made another pitching change; they took out Jergins and tried graduate student Griffin O’Connor. Molinaro popped up for the first out of the 6th inning. Porter was walked and Davis singled. The advancements stopped after Jaconski and DeMucci both flew out.

Penn State also began its defensive 6th inning with a pitching change. Out went Leed and in went lefty Logan Olsen. Hamilton singled to first and Price struck out looking. Olsen walked Larkins and both Price and Larkins stole second and third. Olsen messed up again, hitting Dante Pozzi with a pitch.

After a rough start for Olsen, he got switched out for righty Kyle Emmons. Solomon struck out looking, followed by a groundout from Gilio to end the 6th. The score remained tied 4-4.

O’Connor continued his nice start; he made quick work of the 7th. Yaucher grounded out to second, Mercado did the same, and Anderson went down swinging,

The Nittany Lions made yet another pitching change. This time, they swapped Emmons for Robert Brown III, a freshman right-handed pitcher. Brown III started off well, Ilitch and Peek both grounded out and Sheffield singled. Hamilton got thrown out at first to end the quick 7th; the score was still tied 4-4.

Avery Smith started at-bat for Penn State, and he hit a single. Molinaro and Porter followed up with outs, though Smith got to second. Davis lined out to centerfield for another quick batting performance from Penn State.

Brown III continued his hot start, striking out Price looking. Larkins lined out to right field, but Pozzi hit a home run over left field. Brown III got back on pace with a pop-up out from Solomon to move to the final inning. Georgetown led 5-4.

The Hoyas started the last inning with a pitching change; O’Connor went out for right-handed pitcher Ethan Rucker. Rucker made quick work of the Nittany Lions batters. Jaconski and DeMucci both struck out swinging and Yaucher grounded out to third to end the game.

Up Next

Penn State returns home to face Iowa in its Big Ten opener on Friday at 5:30 p.m.
