Shayne Van Ness came into the match against Ethan Stiles looking for his first Big Ten title.

Each match seemed to have a similar start, with both wrestlers being stagnant, something that continued in this match.

Van Ness hit the high gear, and with just over a minute and a half left in the second period, the junior put Stiles on his back and got the pin for his first-ever Big Ten title.

157 pounds

First place: No. 2 PJ Duke (PSU) vs. No. 1 Antrell Taylor (NEB)

PJ Duke gained a chance at revenge for the only loss of his collegiate career, a close loss to Antrell Taylor in the BJC Dual.

Duke went on the offense early, whether successful or not, and got something he couldn’t get in the previous battle, a takedown.

The second period featured more offense from Duke. Less than a minute into the period, he secured a second takedown, growing his lead to four.

It was more of the same from Duke in the final frame, who scored another takedown. Duke’s offense was on full display, earning the 12-4 major decision to earn his first Big Ten title and avenge his only loss of the season.

165 Pounds

First place: No. 1 Mitchell Mesenbrink (PSU) vs No. 3 Mikey Caliendo (IOWA)

Mitchell Mesenbrink and Mikey Caliendo match up quite frequently. This time it was for the Big Ten title.

Mesenbrink scored a takedown immediately and then another takedown to gain a 6-1 lead. A bow-and-arrow was attempted, but was unsuccessful. The junior controlled the entire match and continued to add points.

Mesenbrink extended his lead to 11-2 before allowing an escape. Caliendo couldn’t get up off the mat, as Mesenbrink gave him no room and was all over the mat. The junior got the victory by major decision, 12-3.

174 pounds

First place: No. 1 Levi Haines (PSU) vs. No. 2 Christopher Minto (NEB)

Levi Haines and Christopher Minto had some controversy at the beginning of the week. Minto was initially the No. 1 seed, with Haines the No. 2 seed. It was challenged and the seeds were reversed, and the only way to find out if the seeds were accurate was to face off in the Big Ten finals.

Neither wrestler found success to start, as attacks were traded. Minto escaped from the bottom in the second period, but was penalized for an illegal hold.

All Haines needed to do in the third period was escape from Minto, which he did early on. Despite multiple attacks both ways, neither wrestler scored and Haines held on to a 2-1 victory.

Haines became the fourth Penn State wrestler all-time to win four Big Ten titles, winning his first two at 157 pounds before jumping two weight classes and earning his final two at 174 pounds.

184 Pounds

First Place: No. 1 Rocco Welsh (PSU) vs No. 2 Max McEnelly (MINN)

Rocco Welsh entered the Big Ten Championships looking for his first title.

The first period didn’t contain a ton of action other than Max McEnelly earning a stalling warning. Welsh had a few takedown attempts and was the more aggressive wrestler throughout the match.

McEnelly tied the match with an escape of his own. A few more attempts by Welsh were countered by a McEnelly attempt, but nothing happened, and the match made its way to tiebreakers. The redshirt sophomore escaped instantly.

There was some question of whether McEnelly got a takedown, but after review, it was ruled no takedown at the end of the first tiebreaker. Welsh was called for stalling, but it didn’t affect the score and the Ohio State transfer came away with the victory.

197 pounds

First place: No. 1 Josh Barr (PSU) vs. No. 2 Camden McDanel (NEB)

Already having beaten Camden McDanel in the BJC earlier this season by major decision, Josh Barr had a rematch with the Cornhusker in the championship bout.

A little under two minutes into the match, Barr secured his first takedown, getting his second arm around McDanel. Barr added a second takedown in the first period, extending his lead to 6-1.

The second period featured another powerful takedown from Barr, sending McDaniel to the mat. Barr led 9-2 entering the third period.

With a chance at bonus points, Barr poured it on in the third period, winning by tech fall. His 100% bonus rate remained intact, earning his first Big Ten title.

285 pounds

Fifth place: No. 4 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs. No. 5 Braxton Amos (WIS)

Cole Mirasola got the victory due to Braxton Amos medically forfeiting.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse continues undefeated start with win over San Diego State
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State has had a clear, sunny start with its new boss, Kayla Treanor, in the 2026 campaign. Yet the new coach and her squad had a daunting task ahead of them: their first West Coast trip of the season, and with the Nittany Lions, as they had a chance to post the program's best start in this millennium.

Ultimately, the blue and white sliced all doubts in half, with an emphatic 18-6 win, which marks Penn State's second consecutive win by such a margin.

The Aztecs arrived into its home turf with a 1-4 record, and from the start, it was clear that the unranked San Diego State would have the firepower to compete with the No.17 Nittany Lions.

Penn State opened up scoring with a pair of unassisted goals, showing no signs of jet lag. The Nittany Lions may have allowed a goal soon after to break the streak, but they went on an emphatic run to pull ahead decisively 9-1, with star Erika Ho netting a goal in the process. Ho had missed the prior few contests due to injury.

The second quarter went more in San Diego State’s way, with it carving into Penn State’s lead to an 11-3 deficit. The blue and white’s star freshman, Rocquette Allen, opened scoring in the third, which only boosted a run to a comfortable 14-4 entering the last frame of action.

Penn State started the fourth quarter with four goals, before the Aztecs netted two in consolation prior to the final buzzer, souring a full team performance for the Nittany Lions.

In the blue and white’s win, Payton Wainman, Maddie Greco, Delaney Radin, Ho and Allen finished with two goals each, while no one surpassed such a figure in assists. In between the pipes, both Sydney Manning and Arden Jansen allowed three goals, with Jansen recording four saves, double that of Manning. Emma Kelly logged a confident 12 draw controls; no one else recorded more than two.

Up Next

Penn State continues its road trip with its first Big Ten opponent of the year, heading to face USC on Thursday, March 12. The action is set for 9 p.m. on the West Coast, with streaming available on Big Ten Plus.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball loses series finale against Texas Tech, pitching struggles
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State looked to leave its road trip to the Lone Star State with a victory against Texas Tech in its series finale on Sunday afternoon. The Nittany Lions lost the two previous games by a large margin, so their pitching simply had to perform better.

Penn State’s pitching woes continued as the squad was routed in seven innings and was swept with a 14-4 loss against Texas Tech.

However, the Red Raiders quickly poured in early runs once again. With the worst team-ERA in the Big Ten at 7.99, the blue and white’s pitchers have barely given its offense a chance at winning games.

Right-hander Isaiah Shayter couldn’t find his control and Penn State’s offense was in another big hole. Tracer Lopez drew a five-pitch one-out walk, then Connor Shouse smacked a double to right, which set up runners on the corners for cleanup hitter Logan Hughes.

The junior delivered with a sneaky double down the right field line, driving in both runners and giving Texas Tech another early 2-0 lead. Shayter escaped the opening frame with only two runs plated, but the Red Raiders scored three more in the second.

The freshman began the inning by walking Davis Rivers and he quickly advanced to third off a single into the left-center gap by Robin Villeneuve. With runners on the corners and still no outs, Kyeler Thompson tapped an infield single to the left side of the diamond and Davis scored.

With a 3-0 lead, the Red Raiders were still searching for more. After both runners advanced into scoring position off a bunt, Shouse plated Villeneuve from third with a fielder’s choice ground ball to shortstop Preston Yaucher.

The bases were loaded after Shayter walked Hughes on four pitches and more offensive small ball scored another run. Linkin Garcia tapped a ball back to Shayter on the mound and he opted to throw Garcia out at first, which extended the Red Raiders’ lead, 5-0.

After Shayter walked the next batter on four pitches, Coach Mike Gambino turned to his bullpen and brought in right-hander Kyle Emmons. The junior stopped the bleeding and forced a fly out to right fielder Jack Porter.

Right-hander Jonny Lowe was cruising through Penn State’s lineup until the third inning. Left fielder Justin Turcovski drew a lead-off walk and Yaucher tapped a one-out infield single. Having a little momentum, center fielder Cohl Mercado walked and designated hitter Michael Anderson knocked a two-RBI single into center.

Texas Tech pulled Lowe and brought in reliever Heeryun Han. The junior retired the next two Penn State batters and preserved the 5-2 lead.

Emmons delivered a pivotal shutdown inning and the Nittany Lions cut further into the Red Raiders’ lead in the fourth. Facing right-hander Kayson Raineri, Porter lifted a lead-off double into the left-center gap.

The sophomore then threw a wild pitch and Porter advanced to third. First baseman Jesse Jaconski grounded out to second baseman Lopez, but Porter plated.

The blue and white were now down just two runs and Emmons retired the Red Raiders in order later in the inning. The offensive momentum kept rolling as Penn State plated another run across in the fifth.

Yaucher lined a lead-off double down the left field line and while the next two Nittany Lions were retired, third baseman Bryce Molinaro delivered with two outs. Texas Tech brought in right-hander Will Jordan to face the biggest bat in Penn State’s lineup.

The Drums, Pennsylvania, native dropped a single into left center and scored Yaucher from second, bringing the Nittany Lions within one run. While the Nittany Lions couldn’t capitalize off an error by the Red Raiders in the sixth, Texas Tech’s offense jumped all over right-hander Dimond Loosli.

The Red Raiders plated four runs in the frame and effectively pulled away from the blue and white. Right-handers Mason Horwat and Ben DeMell couldn’t stop the bleeding either and Texas Tech scored five more runs in the seventh to end the game.

Penn State’s pitching staff surrendered 13 hits and 11 walks, while also recording just three strikeouts. With the sweep, the Nittany Lions fell to 4-9 on the season.

Up Next

Penn State stays on the road and faces Georgetown for a three-game series with the opener at 4 p.m. Tuesday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 3 Penn State Women’s Hockey to battle No. 6 UConn or No. 9 Princeton In NCAA Tournament Regional Final
author: Liana Tiamzon I For the Collegian
description:
The 2026 NCAA Women's Ice Hockey Championship is here, and the Nittany Lions are set to get on the ice. Seeding is pending, but the foes are certain.

Staying 𝐇𝐎𝐌𝐄📍📅 No. 3 Penn State vs. No. 6 UConn/No. 9 Princeton | Saturday, March 14 | 2 p.m. | Pegula Ice Arena#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/Q36tSQCF4O— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 8, 2026

​UConn finished the regular season 27-8-2 and won the Hockey East championship over Northeastern. They will face Penn State for the first time since October 2024, when the teams split a weekend series, If the Huskies beat Princeton.

Princeton closed its regular season at 23-10-0 and secured an at-large bid to the NCAA Tournament. A win over UConn would create a historic matchup. This would mark the first time the Tigers and Nittany Lions have ever faced off.

Penn State secured its fourth straight NCAA Tournament berth after defeating Mercyhurst in the AHA Championship, finishing the season with a program-best 32-5-0 record.

The historic season also earned the Nittany Lions their highest NCAA seed ever and the opportunity to host a regional round at Pegula Ice Arena for the first time in program history.

If Penn State advances through the regional final, it will face the winner of the matchup between No. 2 Wisconsin and Quinnipiac/Franklin Pierce in the Frozen Four on Friday, March 20.

With Pegula Ice Arena serving as the site of this year’s Frozen Four, the Nittany Lions would maintain home-ice advantage for the remainder of the tournament as they chase the first national championship in program history.

Fans are set to watch the game against UConn/ Princeton on March 14th, at 2 pm. At Pegula Ice Arena, University Park and can also be streamed on ESPN+.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Report: Former Penn State punter Blake Gillikin re-signs with Arizona Cardinals
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State specialist is staying in Arizona.

Punter Blake Gillikin has re-signed with the Arizona Cardinals on a one-year deal worth up to $2.65 million, according to Mike Garafolo of NFL Network.

First the kicker, now the punter for the #AZCardinals: Blake Gillikin has agreed to a one-year deal worth up to $2.65M, his agents at @LIFTSPORTSMNGMT tell The Insiders. He missed 12 games with a back injury last season but is healthy now and Arizona moves to retain him ahead of… pic.twitter.com/WtKQziB1zq— Mike Garafolo (@MikeGarafolo) March 8, 2026

Gillikin only played in five games last season due to a back injury. He had 22 punts in 2025, averaging a career-high 51.7 yards per punt.

Next season will be Gillikin's fourth year with the Cardinals. He has punted 271 times in his NFL career, which started in 2021 with the New Orleans Saints.

Gillikin played for Penn State from 2016-19, earning All-Big Ten second team honors in 2017 in addition to honorable mentions in 2016 and 2019.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Braeden Davis, Cole Mirasola compete in Session 3 of Big Ten Championships
author: Andrew Deal & AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
With eight wrestlers in the finals of the Big Ten Championships, the third session was a much quieter one for the Nittany Lions.

Braeden Davis had an opportunity for seventh place, while Cole Mirasola competed for a chance at third place in Session 3.

Here’s how their matches went down on Sunday afternoon.

141 pounds

Seventh place: No. 6 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs. No. 8 Billy Dekraker (NU)

Braeden Davis stepped onto the mat to a loud cheer for his final time this season in the Bryce Jordan Center.

Davis started quickly, taking down Billy DeKraker to secure the first points of the bout.

Leading 3-1, Davis started on top and showcased his strength. For two straight minutes, Davis rode Dekraker, mounting to 2:22 in riding time.

Entering the third period, Dekraker opted for neutral, giving Davis an immediate point. In the midst of a scramble, Davis found himself in position and secured his second takedown of the bout.

Once again, Davis showcased his strength on top, riding out Dekraker to an 8-1 victory. Davis accumulated four minutes of riding time in his victory, earning himself the final NCAA qualifier spot at 141 pounds.

285 pounds

Consolation: No. 4 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs No. 10 Ben Kueter (IOWA)

After a bit of a standstill, Cole Mirasoola had the opportunity to garner the first takedown of the match, but couldn’t get Ben Kueter to fold. Kueter responded with an attempt of his own that was also unsuccessful.

Kueter earned an escape point, but there was no shortage of takedown attempts from Mirasola, but they continued to be unsuccessful.

Mirasola again struggled to get up off the mat from the down position. Kueter maintained the top position for the third period and didn’t allow Mirasola to do anything on offense as Kueter got the victory 2-0.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s basketball closes out regular season with double-digit loss against Rutgers
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State visited Rutgers inside Jersey Mike’s Arena for its regular-season finale with not much at stake as it already clinched last place in the Big Ten standings.

Still, the Nittany Lions lost for the fifth time in their last six games as they endured a 74-62 defeat to the Scarlet Knights on Sunday afternoon, falling to 12-19 overall and 3-17 in conference play.

Penn State managed to force three early turnovers on Rutgers to get out to a 10-8 lead with 15:14 left in the first half. After that, however, it was a different story as the Scarlet Knights went on a 35-11 run to close out the opening 20 minutes with a 43-21 halftime advantage, which is their first halftime lead of 20 points or more in a conference game since 2016, per the Big Ten Network broadcast.

Within that run for Rutgers, the team went on scoring streaks of 16-0 and 12-0, and Penn State endured two scoring droughts that lasted for over five minutes. Late in the first half, forward Josh Reed made a 3-pointer while guard Freddie Dilione V had a pair of baskets, but the Scarlet Knights still held a commanding lead as they shot 56.7% from the field to begin the game.

It was a joint effort for the Scarlet Knights, who forced 10 Penn State turnovers and scored 15 points off of them. Eight players made a basket for Rutgers, with guard Tariq Francis leading the way at 18 points, and the team shot 43.5% across the entire game. Guard Lino Mark had 16 points as well.

The Nittany Lions were not as efficient as they shot 33.3% from the field in the first half before finishing the game at 41.1%. It took until the 2:29 mark of the first half for the team to make a 3-pointer, and it ended up shooting 13.6% from deep.

Dilione and 7-footer Ivan Jurić led Penn State with 16 points each, and guard Kayden Mingo and Reed were each in double figures as well. The Nittany Lions used the interior to generate offense as they scored 32 points in the paint.

Coming out of halftime, Penn State managed to rattle off an 11-2 run with baskets from Mingo, Dilione and guard Dominick Stewart. However, it went on a three-minute field goal drought, and then Jurić got into foul trouble with four fouls with 11:44 left, keeping Rutgers in front, 54-36.

Jurić finished with 5-of-10 shooting from the field and seven rebounds as he made a few quality plays in the paint, but he couldn’t always stay on the floor due to his foul trouble. With Jurić off the floor, the Scarlet Knights kept the Nittany Lions from generating any further runs, leading 63-43 with 7:52 left.

Jurić came back onto the court, and Penn State got points via a pair of free throws by Reed, a layup by forward Mason Blackwood and another layup by Mingo before a long 2-pointer by Jurić made it a 65-51 game with 5:39 left.

But the Nittany Lions, who cut their deficit to as little as 12 points, couldn’t mount another run late in the second half to threaten Rutgers’ lead, and they lost by double digits for the 13th time this season.

Up next

Penn State will enter as the No. 18 seed in the Big Ten Tournament, which runs from March 10-15 inside the United Center in Chicago. The Nittany Lions will face No. 15 seed Northwestern in the first round at approximately 7:30 p.m. Tuesday.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball swept by No. 2 Long Beach State
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State hit the road and headed for sunny California for a three-match stretch against some of the best programs in the country – the first of which was the defending National Champions, No. 2 Long Beach State.

With boots on the ground in The Golden State, the Nittany Lions gave it their all against the Beach but couldn’t get it done, getting hit with a 3-0 sweep in their first match out west.

The blue and white opened up the scoring in the first set, getting off to an early 2-1 lead thanks to a pair of kills from Matthew Luoma and Owen Rose.

The 6-foot-7 Rose was recently awarded the EIVA Defensive Player of the Week for the fifth time in his career. The middle blocker also set a school-record 14 kills against Harvard on Feb. 20.

Backed by two separate 3-1 scoring runs, the Beach quickly overcame the Nittany Lions’ early lead and jumped out to an 11-6 advantage after back-to-back aces from middle blocker Jackson Cryst as well as a triple block play from Skyler Varga, Ben Braun and Wojciech Gajek.

Varga was announced to the AVCA National Collegiate Men's Volleyball Player of the Year Watch List earlier this season.

Gajek recently earned Big West Freshman Of The Week honors after a two-game series against CUI, and entered Saturday’s match against Penn State with 67 kills on the season.

Despite the Beach growing its lead, neither side gave in and points were traded back-and-forth, with Michael Schwob picking up an ace for the blue and white along the way to a 16-16 standstill.

Long Beach State took a timeout when the score was tied at 20-20, with Alex Kandev and Cryst both tallying aces for the Beach, matched by kills from Penn State’s Sean Harvey and Tristan Hassell – both of whom hold the first and second-most kills for the blue and white with 167 and 94, respectively.

It looked like it could go either way right at the end, with both teams sitting at 24 points and needing to reach 26, but the Beach ultimately captured the first set thanks to an ace on the serve from Kandev.

Unlike the first set, Long Beach State got on the board first in the second set by way of error on Schwob’s serve, with the first three points of the set all coming from service errors.

Sophomore middle blocker Gaige Gabriel – who has the third most kills for the blue and white this season – gave the Nittany Lions a 7-5 lead in the second frame with the assist going to Schwob.

The Beach took it personally and went on a 3-0 scoring run to immediately reclaim its lead, capped off with an ace from Gajek.

Again, neither end outright pulled away and kept trading scores, with Jaidyn Bethel and Luoma notching kills for the blue and white to match Braedon Marquardt and Varga’s kills.

Luoma and Bethel hold the fourth and fifth-most kills for Penn State, with Luoma’s 78 and Bethel’s 68.

Long Beach State finally started to extend its lead, creeping ever closer to victory in the second set after back-to-back aces from Marquardt made it a 20-15 lead for the Beach.

Backed by kills from Cryst and Daniil Hershtynovich, the Beach stole the second frame and moved one set closer to sweeping the No. 16-ranked Nittany Lions.

Hershtynovich entered Saturday’s match with the fourth-most kills on Long Beach State, with 65.

The Beach got off to a hot 3-1 start in the early running of the third frame, backed by a Varga kill, a Connor Bloom ace, and a Rose error, but the blue and white fought back and tied the affair at 3-3 thanks to a Rose kill, making up for his prior mistake.

Following the trend from the rest of the match, the third set stayed tight from that point on, with Penn State falling into a deficit but cutting it to 11-10 with the help of several back-to-back errors.

The score remained tied for a while until the Beach went on a 5-2 scoring run backed by two Varga kills to make its advantage 17-15 with the sweep in sight.

An ace from Gajek that gave Long Beach State a two-point advantage looked like a big shot of momentum, but an error on his serve just seconds later sucked the momentum away as Penn State looked to capitalize.

A kill from Harvey just wasn’t enough to turn the tide as the Beach sailed to a third set 25-19 victory thanks to a Cryst ace that sealed the deal on the sweep and put a wrap on the night.

Up Next

Penn State stays on the West Coast for its next two matches, the first of which is against No. 7 Pepperdine at 9 p.m. Wednesday.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s gymnastics falls to Michigan on road due to low scoring day
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State started the season well and had a look to keep the streak rolling over spring break with a trip to Ann Arbor for the Nittany Lions’ resumption of Big Ten play.

Ultimately, the road trip went off without a hitch for the blue and white, as it took home its second loss of the season to Michigan, 326.250-311.550. The final score stood as the second-lowest of Penn State’s season so far, only barely edging out the Nittany Lions' score in their season opener against Army nearly two months ago.

The action started on the floor, with the Wolverines taking over a two-point edge, and Michigan never let go of its lead from there on out. Brayden Erickson led the Nittany Lions’ scoring, but every single Michigan gymnast outscored his 13.400. Charlie Larson started hot for Michigan, as he notched an event high 14.450.

On the pommel horse, the Nittany Lions put up a slightly better team performance. Still, they finished three points under the Wolverines, with Kacper Garnczarek putting up the only performance on an individual level that could compete with Michigan, finishing with a 13.600.

When the rings came up, the Nittany Lions' star Landon Simpson was unable to continue to improve his slow start, with his second straight event finishing with a 12.000, barely any improvement from his 11.800 on the pommel horse. Penn State again fell behind in the event, as Michigan again surpassed the 14-point threshold courtesy of Akshay Puri. After three events, the blue and maize led 162.150-153.550.

Luckily, in the next event, the tide would turn for the Nittany Lions, and they would finish only a point shy of Michigan. Simpson, joined by a pair of teammates with 13.950s, while Erickson logged a 13.850. The Wolverines had a pair of high 14-point scores, but they were unable to expand the Wolverines' lead.

That event marked the only real high spot for Penn State on the day; in the next two events, the Nittany Lions fell. First, a 53.600-49.650 in the parallel bars, then a 54.450-52.700 in the high bar. A pair of Wolverines notched scores over 14 in the events, which was good for six total on the day. The Nittany Lions didn’t post one performance of 14 points or better on the day.

Up Next

Penn State continues with Big Ten play, this time against Michigan’s devout rival, Ohio State. The Nittany Lions return to Rec Hall for the action, and get a long break until action resumes on Sunday, March 22. This will be the first meet this season to be streamed on the Big Ten Network, with a start time of 3 p.m.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling continues dominance, sends 8 wrestlers to finals of Big Ten Championships
author: AJ Piazza & Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State returned to the mats for Session 2 of the Big Ten Championships after holding a comfortable advantage after the opening session, with 81 points.

The Nittany Lions continued that dominance, sending eight wrestlers to the Big Ten Finals, while sitting in first place in the team standings with 146.5 points. Ohio State sat in second place with 112 points.

Here’s how Session 2 went down for Penn State.

125 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Luke Lilledahl (PSU) vs. No. 4 Jacob Moran (IU)

Luke Lilledahl lived up to his “lightning” nickname, getting the first takedown of the match. A minute later, he took down Jacob Moran again, taking a 6-2 lead into the second period.

A third takedown in the second period solidified Lilledahl’s lead, as he rolled to an 11-3 major decision.

Lilledahl’s major decision victory put him back into the Big Ten title bout for the second straight year with a chance to repeat as the conference’s champion at 125.

133 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Marcus Blaze (PSU) vs. No. 5 Drake Ayala (IOWA)

Marcus Blaze and Drake Ayala began at a standstill, as neither wrestler got any offense going in the first period.

Ayala earned an escape point, but both wrestlers struggled with getting takedowns. The match had a slow pace, with neither wrestler actually going down on the mat.

The freshman got the escape point to begin the third period. Both wrestlers continued grappling on their feet, and there were no attempted takedowns throughout the opening three periods, sending the bout to overtime.

Blaze tried to get a takedown to begin the overtime period, but wasn’t successful. The freshman managed to get both legs and secure the only takedown of the match for a 4-1 victory.

141 pounds

Consolations: No. 6 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs. No. 12 Danny Pucino (ILL)

Braeden Davis came out of the gates on fire, notching a takedown within the first 10 seconds, while simultaneously putting Danny Pucino on his back.

Davis worked Pucino on his back, eventually securing the fall at 36 seconds.

Consolations: No. 6 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs. No. 10 Joey Olivieri (RUT)

Davis and Joey Olivieri met in what was initially a first-round matchup before Davis’ seed jumped to No. 6.

Olivieri secured the first takedown after a major scramble and controlled the match from there.

After some head slapping that prompted the referee to talk to Davis, Olivieri was called for stalling, giving Davis a chance to win with a takedown. Despite the opportunity, Davis let up at the end, not giving a full attack attempt in the closing seconds.

Davis’ decision loss places him in the seventh-place bout Sunday afternoon.

149 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Shayne Van Ness (PSU) vs. No. 4 Lachlan McNeil (MICH)

Shayne Van Ness continued the trend of allowing the first takedown to McNeil, as it looked like Van Ness was going to secure the first takedown. McNeil got out of it, however, and got a hold of Van Ness’s legs and got the takedown.

McNeil continued to dominate the opening period and took a 6-1 lead.

Van Ness escaped to close the gap and took down McNeil to lessen the lead to 6-5. It was another slow start for the Nittany Lion, but he started to get into a flow as the match continued.

McNeil got two escapes and Van Ness got two takedowns to take an 11-9 lead with 30 seconds remaining. The junior got the decision victory 11-9.

157 pounds

Semifinals: No. 2 PJ Duke (PSU) vs. No. 3 Kannon Webster (ILL)

In his second consecutive match against a lanky wrestler, PJ Duke took advantage of his shorter, more built frame. Duke tallied the first takedown of the match a little under halfway through the first period.

Duke only needed that single takedown after notching an escape to defeat Kannon Webster, 4-2 and advance to the Big Ten finals in his first season.

165 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Mitchell Mesenbrink (PSU) vs. No. 5 Andrew Sparks (MINN)

Mitchell Mesenbrink went at Andrew Sparks, but Sparks was ready for it, continuing to push at Mesenbrink. Mesenbrink was the first wrestler to score points, but Sparks continued to go at the junior.

Mesenbrink escaped to begin the middle period. The Hartland, Wisconsin, native got his second takedown and almost put Sparks in a bow-and-arrow, but couldn’t secure it. The Nittany Lion led 7-0.

Sparks got his first point of the night, then got thrown down on the mat by Mesenbrink, who acquired his fourth takedown. The sixth takedown was enough and gave Mesenbrink the tech fall, 19-4.

174 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Levi Haines (PSU) vs. No. 4 Carson Kharchla (OSU)

Levi Haines and Carson Kharchla began with a slower start. Neither wrestler found any success in the opening period and entered the second frame knotted at zero.

Haines escaped from the bottom in the second period and successfully took down Kharchla just 48 seconds later.

The Arendtsville, Pennsylvania, native came out victorious, defeating Kharchla 5-2 to advance to the finals.

184 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Rocco Welsh (PSU) vs. No. 4 Brock Mantanona (MICH)

Brock Mantanona came out prepared for Rocco Welsh, getting the first takedown of the match, but the Ohio State transfer escaped. Welsh, meanwhile, had a few opportunities to come away with a takedown, but couldn’t secure it.

Welsh cut the lead to 3-2 with another escape. The Waynesburg, Pennsylvania, native continued with his foot on the gas and got his first takedown of the match and the lead, 5-4.

Mantanona tied the match with an escape, Welsh gained momentum as the match went on and continued takedown attempts, while boos rained down as Mantanona went down on a knee. Welsh got the knee and the takedown in sudden victory to take an 8-5 win.

197 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Josh Barr (PSU) vs. No. 5 Luke Geog (OSU)

After a slow start to the match, Josh Barr ramped things up against Luke Geog. So far this season, Barr has looked invincible, holding a 100% bonus rate.

Barr secured the opening takedown and continued to roll from there. Takedown after takedown came through for Barr as he looked for bonus points once again.

With under a minute to go, the Davison, Michigan, native had the tech fall in sight. A takedown with under 30 seconds to go put him in position and a ride out allowed him to earn the tech fall, 18-3.

Heavyweight

Semifinals: No. 4 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs. No. 1 Taye Ghadiali (MICH)

Cole Mirasola looked like he could’ve gotten a takedown, but it was Taye Ghadiali who got the first takedown. Mirasola escaped and looked like he almost had Ghadiali’s leg, but couldn’t hold on.

The redshirt freshman went down 4-1 early in the middle frame and almost got Ghadiali’s leg, but again couldn’t hold on. Mirasola continued to get chances, but couldn’t finish.

The Nittany Lion struggled to escape most of the final period, but did so late and got a hold of Ghadiali’s leg for the fifth time. He still failed to get the takedown and lost 4-2.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s hockey third line leads 3-2 victory in AHA Championship
author: Steven Pavelka | For the Collegian
description:
The White and Blue’s third line was on fire in the AHA Championship game against the Mercyhurst Lakers, scoring the first two goals in the 3-2 win. This win secured the team's fourth straight AHA title.

Graduate Student Mya Vaslet scored the first goal of the day for the Nittany Lions in period one with a rocket down the center. The goal came off an assist from linemate Matilde Fantin, who won the faceoff directly before her pass to Vaslet.

“In that first period, we got the opening goal, which is really important.” Coach Jeff Kampersal said postgame, “I call Mya ‘M-Train’ but I’m probably going to start calling her ‘Big Game’ because she scores big goals in Championship games.”

The tone-setting goal was the 30th of Vaslet’s career with Penn State and came with some déjà vu.

“It kind of reminded me a little bit of my third year, when I also had the opening goal. I was like, we have to score more than one goal.” Vaslet said, “It was such a stressful game. But then my other linemate, Nicole Hall, also got a goal. So that was pretty exciting, too. I think just coming back from a season-ending injury is pretty emotional and obviously a little nerve-racking, but I was happy that we did get it.”

Vaslet was referencing the second period when sophomore line mate Nicole Hall redirected a shot from Sophie Morrow that gave them a 2-0 lead over the Lakers. Hall has been heating it up recently as she recorded her third goal in the last four games. Vaslet, being the oldest on the team, credits much of Penn State’s success to the younger players who are focused on winning every game they play.

“It's definitely a different feeling than the past years. We have such a great younger group that came in this year, and they were all business.” Vaslet said, “That's actually something that I feel not every team has a chance to be a part of. But with us, it was really cool to see the first year that everyone was locked in for a game and it’s like, Yeah, this is cool and celebrate it, but that's not exactly… we're not done yet.”

Postgame, Mercyhurst coach Michael Sisti credited this Penn State team as being the best the program has ever put on the ice. Many feel the same way and typically credit their depth as the main reason, which has been shown to be true. In a unique season that included the first outdoor game in Beaver Stadium and a stretch of losing key players to the Olympics, the depth and team chemistry have proven to be key factors in their success.

With Penn State securing home-ice advantage after this win and their depth just getting better as the season progresses, they remain a frontrunner to win it all.

This was the fifth season matchup between the two teams in a series that Penn State won 4-1. The lone loss to the Lakers came in an overtime thriller back in January.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball falls in mercy-rule loss to Texas Tech in second game of series
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State returned to Dan Law Field at Rip Griffin Park in Lubbock on Saturday, looking for redemption in Game 2 of its first-ever series against Texas Tech.

The Nittany Lions suffered another loss, putting their record at 4-8, in a mercy-rule victory by the Red Raiders in the seventh inning, ending 21-6.

Penn State started strong with centerfielder Cohl Mercado hitting his first home run of the season, only to be met by Texas Tech scoring seven runs in the bottom of the first, putting them at a substantial lead.

The Nittany Lions responded in the second with a run from Jack Porter, after Jesse Jaconski’s double to left-center left him the perfect opportunity to score from first base, only for the team to be struck out soon after. They let up another four runs from the Red Raiders in the ensuing inning.

Penn State tried to make a comeback in the third with Michael Anderson, who’s been a strong leader for the blue and white, homering himself and Mercado home, to close the gap, 11-4.

They were once again met with another run scored by Tracer Lopez on a fielder’s choice hit to third, keeping them six runs behind.

The Red Raiders continued to threaten the Nittany Lions, putting up two more runs, making it 14-4 in the top of the fifth. Soon after, Kyeler Thompson walked from a hit-by-pitch, letting another run in by Caden Ferraro before Penn State switched up its pitchers from Logan Olson to Ethan Bauerschmidt.

Entering the fifth, Bauerschmidt was quickly relieved by Ryan Free for three hits before Logan Olson came back in, giving Texas Tech their 15th run of the day in the fifth, before being swapped out with Braeden Leed. The Nittany Lions were still met with two more runs scored on a single to left field.

Trying to close the gap in the sixth, Avery Smith and Porter both scored runs, closing the deficit to 17-6, before striking out with the bases loaded. Texas Tech kept hammering in runs as

Connor Shouse walked from another hit-by-pitch, before Logan Hughes blasted a home run into deep right field, enabling three RBIs, putting it at a 20-6 lead. Ryan Coleman ended the game with a hit in the 21st and final run for the Red Raiders.

Texas Tech defeated Penn State 21-6 with 18 hits, two home runs and six walks, putting on a tough offensive front for the Nittany Lions.

Up next

Penn State will face off in its last game of the series against Texas Tech at 2 p.m. EST Sunday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Lehman’s career day pushes Aquino-less Penn Sate men's lacrosse past No. 9 Cornell
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
Kyle Lehman has been a steady presence for Penn State men’s lacrosse’s attack unit all season, but his performances can largely go under the radar due to sophomore phenom Hunter Aquino sharing a line with him.

On Saturday against No. 9 Cornell, however, Aquino was sidelined for the first time this season due to an unspecified injury. Lehman made the best out of his teammate’s absence.

The Ambler, Pennsylvania native notched seven points in the victory over the Big Red, a mark that surpassed his prior career high of five.

“Without having Hunter on the field today, it was a little bit of a question mark for people outside of the team,” Lehman said. “But inside the team, we all knew that we just had to play to our abilities and play ‘we’ ball, not ‘me’ ball.”

Lehman found the back of the net six times on Saturday, giving fits to Cornell goaltender Matthew Tully. The Big Red netminder finished with a .296% save percentage, making for a rough day at the office for the junior.

That total also set a new collegiate best for the junior attackman, as his sock trick doubled his prior best of three. After Saturday’s game, Lehman leads the Nittany Lions with 22 points and 15 goals.

“We have so many guys out there that can score,” Lehman said. “I credit all my teammates today for the success that not only I had, but that the whole team had.”

Saturday’s mark of 19 goals is the Nittany Lions’ highest total on the campaign, and their most since last year’s win over Robert Morris. Lehman attributes Saturday’s blowout win to the week of preparation that preceded it.

“Credit to the guys off the field today,” Lehman said. “We call them the bomb squad, the guys that prepare the starting offense and starting defense, they give us hell throughout the week. They make the drills ten times harder for us in practice so that when we get to game day, we’re just ready to go.”

As much as he’d like to credit others, Lehman’s performance, particularly early in the game, was instrumental in the victory on Saturday, something that coach Jeff Tambroni noted.

“Faraone and Lehman got us off to a great start,” Tambroni said. “Taking good, quality shots and having good, quality possessions early in the game creates that confidence and momentum that we’ve utilized in the last couple of games.”

Penn State’s offense as a whole was strong in the win, but stronger still was the performance by the Nittany Lion attack unit. The trio of Lehman, Liam Matthews and Jack Iannantuono contributed 12 of the 19 goals, with depth piece Enzo Grieco adding another late in the fourth quarter.

Even without Aquino, the attackmen’s chemistry is one of the driving forces behind the success that Penn State has found throughout the first half of the regular season.

“We’re very tight,” Lehman said. “We try to get lunch by ourselves once a week, and I love breaking bread with them. It's a good dynamic. They’re fun kids to be around, and even when we’re on the field, we’re having conversations that may not include lacrosse.”

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: No. 24 Penn State softball loses second straight against No. 11 Virginia Tech
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another big test presented itself for No. 24 Penn State when the Nittany Lions took on their third-ranked opponent of the season against No. 11 Virginia Tech. Following an upset loss to JMU earlier in the day, a win over the Hokies would be huge for the blue and white in the rankings.

Despite leading early, the blue and white fell for its second straight game in a 7-6 loss against Virginia Tech.

Though it wouldn’t be easy, as Virginia Tech entered the game on an 11-game winning streak and an overall record of 18-2. After the Nittany Lions couldn’t produce much offense in their loss to JMU, they bounced back early against the Hokies.

Penn State jumped on right-hander Bree Carrico and plated three runs in the opening frame. Center fielder Natalie Lieto poked a one-out single to left and third baseman Brooke Klosowicz quickly drove her in off a double.

The bases were soon loaded for the blue and white after a few walks and first baseman Gabby Gradishar then drew a two-out walk of her own, extending Penn State’s lead 2-0. Carrico simply lost control and walked her fourth batter of the inning, designated player Madison Seidel.

Right-hander Bridget Nemeth now had an early 3-0 cushion to work with against a tough Virginia Tech squad. The Hokies quickly scored three runs of their own in the bottom half of the first. Michelle Chatfield led off with a double into the right-center gap and Kylie Aldridge followed with a double of her own to left center, scoring Chatfield.

Just when it seemed like Nemeth could escape the inning with just one run scored, Jordan Lynch lunched a two-run home run to left center and knotted the score at three. Yet, the blue and white’s offense took the lead right back against left-hander Emma Mazzarone in the second.

Klosowicz started some two-out magic with a single squeezed down the left field line. Catcher Macy Chamberlin then smashed a two-run home run over the right field wall and gave the Nittany Lions a 5-3 lead.

Left fielder Breanna Hanik reached base off an error by shortstop Annika Rohs, but she couldn’t score off the double by second baseman Michela Barbanente.

After Nemeth surrendered a double by Rachel Castine and a walk, Coach Clarisa Crowell brought in right-hander Abigail Britton for relief. The LaVale, Maryland, native struck out Chatfield and forced a flyout to end the threat.

Both squads were kept quiet in the third, but the fourth brought about scoring opportunities for the two offenses. Klosowicz knocked a lead-off single through the left side and then advanced all the way to third off two wild pitches thrown by Mazzarone.

Chamberlain walked and presented Hanik with runners on the corners and no outs. However, the freshman struck out swinging. Barbanente’s ground ball led to Klosowicz being thrown out at home and Penn State was held scoreless in the inning.

A pair of solo shots tied the game at five later in the fifth. Addison Foster started the scoring off with a lead-off home run to right center and Castine powered a one-out home run the same way.

The game was back and forth throughout as the Nittany Lions took the lead again in the fifth, but the Hokies struck right back. Right fielder Cara Bohner drew a four-pitch walk and then advanced to second off Mazzarone’s third wild pitch.

Third baseman Allison Oneacre also drew a walk, but Lieto followed with a strikeout. However, Klosowicz poked a routine ground ball to second baseman Castine, but she muffled it and allowed Bohner to score.

Now down 6-5, Virginia Tech tied the game back up in the bottom half of the sixth. Crowell turned to left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris to preserve the tight lead. Yet, Zoe Yaeger pulled a solo home run to left field and made the score 6-6.

The Hokies continued pressuring Kellepouris after a single and a walk, but the freshman kept her composure and limited the damage to just one run. However, freshman Avery Layton swiftly retired the Nittany Lions in the sixth and Virginia Tech took its first lead of the game.

Mackenzie Duncan started the sixth as Penn State’s fourth pitcher of the contest. Castine was a thorn for the blue and white’s pitching. The senior smashed her second home run of the game to left and gave the Hokies a late 7-6 lead.

Penn State wasn’t able to rally in the final inning despite Gradishar hitting a lead-off single and fell for its second straight game. The Nittany Lions finished 2-2 at the Hokie Invite and now possess an 18-6 record.

Up Next

Penn State travels cross-country for its last game before conference play against Oregon State at 8 p.m. Wednesday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘Katie’s group has come in and won’ | Goalies duel in No. 3 Penn State’s AHA Championship win
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Goaltending wins championships, especially in a sport like ice hockey where a goalie faces consistent pressure throughout 60 minutes of play. In a one-and-done championship game, a netminder’s performance under pressure is crucial to taking home the trophy.

No. 3 Penn State and No. 12 Mercyhurst possess the top two goaltenders in the Atlantic Hockey America (AHA) conference—senior Katie DeSa and sophomore Magdalena Luggin. DeSa, the two-time reigning AHA Goaltender of the Year, was also named to the All-AHA First Team while Luggin was named to the All-AHA Second Team.

Both elite netminders competed in the AHA Championship game at Pegula Ice Arena at 2 p.m. on Saturday, with the Nittany Lions as the No. 1 seed and the Lakers holding the No. 2 seed. It was the fourth-consecutive meeting between the teams in the conference finale and the final collegiate meeting between DeSa and Luggin on opposing ends of the ice.

“Every year (Mercyhurst has) gotten better,” DeSa said. “Emotions get high. So each year we beat them, it’s a little fuel to their fire. This was probably the best game that we played against them and the best game they played against us.”

Although both goaltenders saw ample shots, Luggin had the tougher afternoon as the blue and white dominated possession time. Penn State came away with a four-peat of the AHA Championship, defeating Mercyhurst, 3-2.

“(DeSa’s senior class) has come in here and won and that’s the only thing that they’ve known to do,” coach Jeff Kampersal said.

DeSa closed the game with 29 saves on 31 shots for a .935 save percentage, while Luggin finished with 30 saves on 33 shots, totalling a .909 save percentage. Luggin closed conference play with a 17-10-3 record as DeSa improved to 26-3 heading into the NCAA Tournament.

Luggin — experiencing the busier contest — had her head constantly on a swivel, searching for open sight lanes as the Nittany Lions played an overload structure to set screens. Despite making many saves through traffic, Katelyn Roberts’ power-play goal found a way through her five-hole to take a 3-0 lead.

The most effective tool in her arsenal was her glove. From the opening puck drop, Luggin tracked the puck cleanly into her catcher and allowed her defenders to rest by holding on for a whistle.

“You always need a great goalie,” Mercyhurst coach Michael Sisti said. “We’ve always had it in our program. Luggin has been great. She gave us a chance to win, made some amazing saves, so it didn’t get away from us.”

DeSa, on the other hand, primarily utilized her superior positioning to make saves with her chest, stick and leg pads. This inherently caused more rebounds; however, DeSa routinely directed the puck to the corners away from the net-crashing Lakers.

The lone goal that evaded DeSa’s ability was early in the third period on a back-handed wraparound by Abby Poitras that found the back of the net past her outstretched left skate on the far side.

The Pawcatuck, Connecticut, native also used intimidation tactics after freezing the puck in her crease, shoving any Mercyhurst forwards that overstayed their welcome around her net.

Midway through the third period, both netminders faced the most chaotic series of events of the game, as each team attempted to score on breakaways.

First, Sophie McKinley broke free of Penn State’s defense, but DeSa slid into a chest save before the Nittany Lions went the other way. Grace Outwater and Mikah Keller were denied by Luggin’s pad on a 2-on-0 that Mercyhurst immediately turned around for a 2-on-1. DeSa made the second save with her arm to keep the score 3-1.

“That was really chaotic for everyone,” DeSa said. “But I knew that I had to lock in and make those saves.”

After pulling Luggin, the Lakers swarmed DeSa. Although the netminder shaved time off the clock with a scrambling save, Mercyhurst’s Berlin Lolacher tapped in a loose rebound past DeSa to put the game within one.

However, DeSa remained locked in. She was quick to cover anything that came near her crease and assisted the Nittany Lions in securing their fourth AHA title.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: No. 3 Penn State women’s hockey defeats No. 12 Mercyhurst, wins fourth straight AHA championship
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
A season full of historic milestones and the program being represented in the Olympics by three Olympians culminated in an AHA championship rematch against Mercyhurst.

The Nittany Lions went 3-1 against the Lakers in the regular season, suffering an upset loss in overtime in their final meeting of the season. However, the blue and white looked to put the conference loss aside and defend its title in front of their fans at Pegula Ice Arena.

In the fourth-straight championship game between the two teams, Penn State won its fourth title after a dominant display from the second line led to a 3-2 final.

First period

Magdalena Luggin was forced to flash the leather in the early stages of the game for Mercyhurst. Penn State continued to generate multiple offensive rushes in the first five minutes of the game, setting the tone early.

Following several chances from the Nittany Lions, a feeling-out process stemmed between the two teams. Several cross-ice passes resulting in an icing plagued the two sides, leaving minimal chances at both ends.

That was until Matilde Fantin won a key offensive zone faceoff. The Como, Italy, native immediately fed a pass to Mya Vaslet in front of the net for the first goal of the game. This was the graduate student’s 30th career goal.

In what was a disciplined, penalty-free game from both squads, Tessa Janecke committed the first penalty of the game 17 minutes in.

Penn State’s penalty kill unit stood strong, defending every breakout rush with suffocating pressure on the puck carrier, successfully ending the man advantage.

The horn soon sounded after the kill with the Nittany Lions ahead 1-0.

Second period

It was Katie DeSa’s turn to face instant offensive pressure as Mercyhurst came out firing to begin the second period. The Pawcatuck, Connecticut, native positioned herself with the shooter and made a few pad saves to keep the score 1-0.

Penn State generated chances as the middle frame neared its halfway point. Abby Stonehouse found herself one-on-one with a defender. The Bienheim, Ontario, native attempted to toe drag past the defenseman; however, was quickly shut down before getting past the skates.

Most of the first ten minutes were played closely by both defenses. The two squads had an answer for every threat of pressure put on by the opposition.

However, the blue and white were able to cash in on a quick developing play in its offensive goal. Sophie Morrow sent a wrist shot from the top of the faceoff circle that was redirected by Nicole Hall. The Stockholm, Sweden, native beat the left pad of Luggin to make the score 2-0.

The third line was responsible for the first two goals for Penn State in the championship game.

The Nittany Lions weren’t done scoring. After a penalty by Payten Evans put Penn State on the power play, Katelyn Roberts ripped a wrist shot from the slot that went five-hole and into the net, extending the lead to 3-0.

The horn sounded, putting an end to a two-goal second period for the Nittany Lions. 20 minutes remained in the championship game.

Third period

The Lakers opened up the final period of play, scoring their first goal. Abby Poitras found the loose puck behind DeSa’s net and scored a wrap-around goal.

Penn State tightened up defensively after letting up the early third-period goal. That was until miscommunication on the blue line led to a breakaway for Sophie McKinley that was denied by DeSa.

This opportunity for Mercyhurst sprouted chances on both sides of the ice as the pace of play began to ramp up with under 10 minutes left in the game.

DeSa had to remain dominant in net to hold off the late surge of offense produced by the Lakers. Mercyhurst directed a total of 16 shots at the netminder in the final period.

Luggin was pulled from her net with just under three minutes left in the game. The Lakers had two minutes to net two goals in order to keep their championship hopes alive.

It was Berlin Lolacher who scored the second goal for Mercyhurst with just over a minute left, shortening the lead to one for the blue and white.

However, it was too little too late as the Penn State faithful rose to their feet after the final 60 seconds began to fade off the clock. The horn sounded and the Nittany Lions were AHA champions once again.

Up Next

Penn State will play in the quarterfinal round of the NCAA Women’s Ice Hockey tournament on March 14.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball’s offense falters, loses first game at Hokie Invite against JMU
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After going 2-0 on day one of the Hokie Invite, No. 24 Penn State had an opportunity to carry its momentum against JMU on Saturday afternoon. The Dukes entered the contest at 10-10 and were fresh off a win over Bryant earlier in the day.

Despite being red-hot offensively this season, the Nittany Lions’ bats were mostly shut down and they fell 5-3 against JMU.

Right-hander McKenna Young started her second game in two days and swiftly retired JMU’s batters in the opening frame. One of the keys to the blue and white’s early-season success is striking first and the squad continued that trend.

The scoring started with right-hander Payton List hitting leadoff shortstop Kaylie Walters. Center fielder Natalie Lieto then smashed a double into right center and scored Walters from first. Despite Penn State leaving Lieto stranded in scoring position, Young now worked with an early 1-0 lead.

However, it wouldn’t take long for the Dukes to knot the game right back up as Bella Henzler led off the second with a solo shot into left. A couple of batters later, Kendra Lewis blasted a ground-rule double into left-center. Yet, Young minimized the damage and JMU settled for just one run in the inning.

The Nittany Lions went down quietly in their half of the second with List picking up two strikeouts and coach Clarisa Crowell turned to her bullpen in the third. Right-hander Abigail Britton delivered a pivotal shutdown inning and Penn State’s bats soon went to work.

Lieto led off the bottom of the third frame with a bang and smothered a solo dinger over the batter’s eye into center, giving Penn State a 2-1 lead. Yet, the Dukes took the lead right back in the fourth.

Emily LeGrand and Bella Henzler started the momentum with back-to-back singles. List then helped herself on the mound and dropped a single into right. Right fielder Cara Bohner fumbled the play, which plated LeGrand and allowed List to advance into scoring position.

With runners on second and third and still no outs, Britton was in a jam. However, the LaVale, Maryland, native allowed just one more run to the plate. After Lewis struck out, Kira Ortega gave JMU the 3-2 lead off a sacrifice fly to center.

After the blue and white couldn’t get much offense going, Crowell brought in left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris. The Olathe, Kansas, native allowed a few singles in the fifth, but kept the deficit to just one run.

Bar a few bumps early, List was dealing against a tough Penn State offense. Later in the fifth, Lieto drew a one-out walk, then advanced to second off a wild pitch and third baseman Allison Oneacre followed with a walk of her own. However, Lieto was left stranded at third as Penn State’s big bats couldn’t knock her in.

The Dukes knocked in a pivotal insurance run in the sixth. Ortega lifted a solo home run to left center and extended JMU’s lead, 4-2. Meanwhile, the Nittany Lions were held scoreless once again in the bottom half of the inning.

Now in the late innings, Penn State felt the pressure down two runs. First baseman Gabby Gradishar led off with a walk and then advanced to second on a wild pitch. However, she was caught stealing third by JMU’s catcher Henzler.

While the Nittany Lions failed to get much going offensively, the Dukes added another run to their lead in the seventh. Cali Legzdin reached on an error by Walters at shortstop and Henzler knocked her in with a one-out single to left field.

The blue and white put together a rally in its last breath, but ultimately came up short and fell to 18-5 on the season with the loss. Lieto doubled down the left field and Oneacre scored her with a single into left center, but List didn’t falter and closed the game out.

Up Next

Penn State takes on its third-ranked opponent of the season against No. 11 Virginia Tech on Saturday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey earns commitment from 2008-born forward William Yared
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has added a piece to its future.

2008-born forward William Yared announced his commitment to the Nittany Lions on Saturday.

‼️🚨BREAKING🚨‼️The Penn State Nittany Lions have received a commitment from William Yared!🦁🦁🦁The ‘08 born Forward from Quebec has 25 points in 46 GP during the 2025-26 season for @SJSeaDogs Ranked the #163 NA skater by @NHLCentralScout for the 2026 NHL Draft pic.twitter.com/COwbUSI9Yr— College Puck NXT (@CollegePuckNXT) March 7, 2026

Yared is ranked as a 4.5-star recruit and is ranked by NHL Central Scouting as the No. 163 North American skater in the 2026 NHL Entry Draft. He is from Quebec, Canada.

The forward currently plays for the Saint John Sea Dogs of the Quebec Maritimes Junior Hockey League (QMJHL). Yared has 10 goals and 15 assists in 46 games played this season.

Yared, 18, could join Penn State as early as next year.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men's basketball to hire Calvin Booth in a consulting role
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is reportedly set to bring in an alum with NBA front office experience.

The Nittany Lions are hiring Calvin Booth in a consulting role to assist with roster management, according to Jon Sauber of the Centre Daily Times.

Source: Penn State men's basketball is hiring former Denver Nuggets GM Calvin Booth to assist with roster management.More here:https://t.co/f1jtDMwTr8— Jon Sauber (@JonSauber) March 7, 2026

Booth was the general manager for the Denver Nuggets from 2020-25. He was a part of the 2023 Nuggets team that won the NBA championship, but his contract wasn't extended after the 2024-25 season.

The former Nittany Lion was drafted in the second round of the 1999 NBA draft by the Washington Wizards. He played in the NBA until 2009, making stops with the Dallas Mavericks, Seattle SuperSonics, Milwaukee Bucks, Philadelphia 76ers, Minnesota Timberwolves and Sacramento Kings as well.

Booth, who played in Happy Valley from 1995-99, is Penn State's all-time career leader in blocks with 428. He made 114 appearances with the Nittany Lions, averaging 11.3 points and 6.4 rebounds per game across his career.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 14 Penn State dominates No. 9 Cornell, continues strong Ivy League run
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
After dominating Yale last weekend, No. 14 Penn State men’s lacrosse continued its Ivy League slate on Saturday, matching up with reigning national champion No. 9 Cornell at Panzer Stadium.

In their last meeting, the Big Red eliminated the Nittany Lions in the semifinals of last season’s NCAA tournament, where the blue and white fell 11-9 at Gillette Stadium in Foxborough, Mass.

The meeting also held a deeper meeting for coach Jeff Tambroni, who coached Cornell for ten seasons before coming to Penn State in 2010.

Tambroni would dominate his former team, beating the Big Red 19-7.

After a big save by Preston Hawkins, Kyle Lehman worked his defender, driving in front of the cage and burying the ball in the net to put Penn State up 1-0 early.

Following the ensuing faceoff, midfielder Michael Faraone drove over the top and fired a shot past Big Red goaltender Matthew Tully to double the blue and white’s advantage.

With just under nine minutes remaining in the quarter, John Jude Considine split down the alley and worked in front of the Cornell net, where he finished to put the Nittany Lions up 3-0.

Less than a minute later, Lehman found himself wide open on the left side of the net, allowing him to wind up and power a low shot past Tully for the second time, forcing a Cornell timeout.

Liam Matthews gave Penn State a 5-0 advantage with a bouncing shot that found the nylon as the Canadian attackman fell to the turf.

Moments later, Jon King picked off a Big Red pass and sprinted downfield, finding Jack Iannantuono on the fastbreak, the freshman firing a shot into the top right corner of the net to stretch the Nittany Lion lead to six.

After that score by Iannantuono, Lehman scored again on a nearly identical look to his second goal, securing the first-quarter hat trick with a shot from Tully’s left side.

Iannantuono scored his second goal of the game early in the second quarter following a great find from Chase Robertson, putting the blue and white up 8-0.

Cornell finally got on the board with 10:22 remaining in the quarter, with Ryan Waldman driving to the crease and finishing past Hawkins, cutting the lead to seven.

The Nittany Lions got it right back, however, when Iannantuono cut from behind the net and found Lehman, who matched his jersey number with his fourth goal of the contest.

Lehman’s career day continued after he received a feed from Andrew Beard on the doorstep of the Cornell crease, and finished again for his fifth goal of the game.

The Big Red got their second when Matthew Perfetto fired a shot past Hawkins with a flag down on the field, a penalty that would stay on the board due to its major classification.

That penalty would bear no fruit for the Big Red, and the game went into the half with Penn State leading 10-2.

Cornell opened the second-half scoring with a goal by Willem Firth on the end of a behind-the-back feed by Ryan Goldstein. Penn State mounted a quick response with Beard scoring on a close shot assisted by Robertson.

Firth got another for the Big Red, cutting the lead to seven on a pass from Brian Luzzi. Not long after, Lehman secured his sock trick on an unassisted goal that doubled his previous career high of three goals.

Senior defenseman Alex Ross got in on the Penn State point-scoring, assisting Matthews on a goal that the attackman would finish underhand.

Faraone scored his second of the contest with a wraparound dodge from behind the cage, tucking the ball past Tully to bring the Nittany Lion lead to ten goals. He got his hat trick soon after on another unassisted goal that brought the score to 15-4.

A turnover in the defensive zone put King on another fastbreak, then worked the ball to Lehman, who assisted Matthews to give the Canadian his hat trick as well.

The goals kept flowing in the late stages of the third quarter, as Beard found the back of the net on a low shot fed by Matthews to bring the lead to 13 as the frame expired.

Iannantuono joined in on the hat trick trend with a wicked low-to-high shot on the run that got past Tully and tucked under the crossbar.

The seven-goal Nittany Lion run was ended by Perfetto, and followed soon after with another Cornell goal by Luke Robinson.

Redshirt attackman Enzo Grieco scored his first collegiate goal on a feed from Wesley Scurci, a duo both produced by Mars Area High School just outside of Pittsburgh.

In the dying moments of the fourth quarter, Cornell got a goal from Goldstein, cutting the score to 19-7. That score would hold until the final buzzer, as Penn State secured a statement win over a strong opponent.

Next Up

The Nittany Lions will head to Charlotte for a neutral-site matchup with No. 6 North Carolina on Saturday at 6:30 p.m.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling begins Big Ten Championships with successful first session
author: Andrew Deal & AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 1 Penn State began its quest for another Big Ten title, this time in its home, the Bryce Jordan Center.

During the first session, nine Nittany Lions advanced to Saturday night’s semifinals, with the other still holding a chance to earn an auto bid to the NCAA Championships.

The Nittany Lions finished with 81 points in the opening session, leading the pack heading into Session 2.

Here’s how the first part of Day 1 went down for the Nittany Lions.

125 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Luke Lilledahl (PSU) vs. No. 8 Dean Peterson (Iowa)

Luke Lilledahl stepped on the mat to a roar from the Penn State faithful and gave his fans a show.

After a couple of scrambles in the first period, Lilledahl tallied the first takedown in the second frame to regain the lead. The Nittany Lion controlled the match the rest of the way, notching another takedown in the third period.

Riding out Peterson, Lilledahl held the Hawkeye in the air and dropped him back down to the mat, solidifying his decision victory, 8-3.

133 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Marcus Blaze (PSU) vs. No. 9 Dylan Shawver (RUT)

Marcus Blaze started as the aggressor in the match, as he secured the first takedown of the match and took an early lead.

The freshman escaped to extend his lead in the middle period and continued to take down Shawver. That cycle repeated in the third period for Blaze, who continued to be the leader in action. The Perrysburg, Ohio, native earned four takedowns in the match.

Blaze dominated his way through Shawver, earning a major decision 16-3.

141 pounds

First round: No. 6 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs. No. 11 Dario Lemus (MD)

Braeden Davis kicked things off for the Nittany Lions, wrestling Dario Lemus in the opening round.

Both wrestlers started with a feeling out process, with neither scoring points. Davis notched the first takedown part way through the opening frame, but got himself into a precarious situation. Lemus took advantage getting four nearfall points with Davis fighting to stay off his back.

Trailing 4-3, Davis immediately followed with a reversal to regain the lead. However, with just a 5-4 lead, he’d need to ride out Lemus. He did just that to end the second period, before riding Lemus for the entirety of the third, securing a 6-4 decision. His decision put the first points on the board for the Nittany Lions.

Quarterfinals: 141: No. 6 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs. No. 3 Vance Vombaur (MINN)

Braeden Davis conceded the first takedown of the match to Vance Vombaur. Davis responded with a takedown of his own, taking the lead. However, that was the extent of his offense.

Vombaur answered Davis’ takedown with one of his own before getting him on his back for four nearfall points. Trailing 11-5 entering the third period, Davis needed points quickly. However, Vombaur did exactly what he did in the second period, scoring a takedown and getting four more swipes for nearfall points.

Vombaur rolled to a 20-5 tech fall victory, sending Davis into the consolations.

149 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Shayne Van Ness (PSU) vs. No. 8 Ryder Block (IOWA)

Shayne Van Ness and Ryder Block started off even. Both wrestlers took shots and it started the same way it did in their last match together, with Block earning the first successful takedown and leading 3-0.

Van Ness started to ramp up in the second period with an escape and a point due to a stall from Block. Block continued to stall and lost the match because of it, with Van Ness earning the victory 5-4 without any takedowns.

157 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 2 PJ Duke (PSU) vs No. 7 Brandon Cannon (OSU)

A matchup that was hyped up to be a title match, whether at the Big Ten Championships or the National Championships, took place in the quarterfinals.

Brandon Cannon found success early, taking down PJ Duke to secure the first points of the match. Duke, however, responded with a takedown of his own before notching another one before the end of the first period.

The strength of the Nittany Lion was on full display throughout the final two periods, as he rode Cannon for a good amount of time, notching a takedown in the second period before going on full attack in the third. Duke rolled to a major decision victory, 20-7, after three takedowns in the final frame.

165 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Mitchell Mesenbrink (PSU) vs No. 8 Paddy Gallagher (OSU)

Mitchell Mesenbrink did Mesenbrink things. The junior started off with a takedown after Paddy Gallagher had a few unsuccessful attempts. Mesenbrink got the takedown on Gallagher and rode him out for the rest of the first period.

There wasn’t much action from Gallagher throughout the rest of the match, as Mesenbrink didn’t really let him find a way to break through.

The junior continued to be on top of Gallagher and dominated the match with a 10-2 major decision victory.

174 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Levi Haines (PSU) vs. No. 9 Collin Kelly (ILL)

Levi Haines proved he’s the top wrestler in the country against Collin Kelly, as he scored the first takedown of the match.

The second period saw double of the first, as Haines notched two more takedowns and picked up a point from Kelly stalling. In the third, the Nittany Lion secured two final takedowns to get the tech fall win.

184 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Rocco Welsh (PSU) vs. No. 8 Angelo Ferrari (IOWA)

The boo birds came out when Angelo Ferrari took a knee, while the first period was a whole lot of nothing between him and Rocco Welsh. Ferrari took his knee, and Welsh couldn’t find a way to attack.

Ferrari earned an escape point and then whiffed on an attempted takedown on Welsh, who showed elite defense and didn’t allow Ferrari to get him down on the mat. The sophomore earned an escape and knotted it up at one.

Ferrari had a few attempts to takedown Welsh, but Welsh found a way out each time.

Welsh got the riding time over Ferrari and got the victory in tiebreakers 2-1.

197 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Josh Barr (PSU) vs. No. 8 Gabe Sollars (IND)

Josh Barr began his match quickly with an early takedown of Gabe Sollars. Throughout the rest of the first frame, Barr took down Sollars three more times, getting out to a 12-3 lead.

The second period featured more of the same, as Barr ended the match early after three takedowns, winning by tech fall, 21-6.

Heavyweight

First round: No. 4 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs No. 13 Gabe Christenson (NU)

Cole Mirasola attacked from the start, securing a takedown and getting the pin on Gabe Christenson in 25 seconds.

Quarterfinals: No. 4 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs No. 5 Braxton Amos (WIS)

Cole Mirasola has had a trend of starting out in front and that continued in this match. Mirasola secured a takedown over the much larger Braxton Amos and took a 3-1 lead into the middle period.

The second period lacked action, with neither wrestler doing much offensively.

Mirasola escaped and extended his lead 4-1. Amos added an escape point in the third period, but couldn’t do anything else, leading to a 4-2 victory for Mirasola.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey's Reidler, Fleming deliver juxtaposing performances in loss to No. 11 Wisconsin
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following a 7-3 loss to No. 11 Wisconsin on Thursday night, No. 6 Penn State suffered another lopsided defeat to the Badgers on Friday as it fell 5-2.

“They fought until the end,” Guy Gadowsky said. “That was important, because last night, that’s not what happened.”

Sophomore goaltender Kevin Reidler started the game strong, allowing a single goal in the first period and saving 14 shots from the Badgers.

The second period, however, was a much different story.

Less than 30 seconds into the frame, a shot from Gavin Morrissey ricocheted off of Cade Christenson’s back and past the Swedish netminder.

Just over a minute after the score from Morrissey, a strong feed from sophomore forward Ryan Botterill found linemate Oliver Tulk, who was waiting just in front of Reidler and tucked the puck past him.

And again, Wisconsin found its third goal in the first five minutes of the period when Blake Montgomery powered a one-timer from the Big Ten logo past the Penn State goaltender.

After the Badgers’ fourth consecutive goal to begin the matchup, Reidler was pulled by Gadowsky in favor of freshman Josh Fleming. The outing marked Reidler’s third straight game allowing four or more goals, and the second poor defensive performance in a row for the blue and white.

“The hook was made entirely to change the mojo,” Gadowsky said. “(He took it) like a champ. He said ‘I totally get it.’”

In relief, Fleming put in a shift that starkly contrasted that of Reidler. The Montreal, Quebec, native made 15 saves, good for a .941 save percentage on the evening.

“Flem’s one of the best goalies in the country,” forward Ben Schoen said.

Schoen’s point is valid, as Fleming ranked fifth in the country with a .931 save percentage entering the weekend, though that mark dropped to .924 after the Wisconsin series.

He’s had his struggles in recent games, including Thursday’s meeting with Wisconsin, where he was scored on seven times. He’s given up three or more goals in five of his last six appearances dating back to the January series with Michigan State.

Gadowsky declined to name his initial playoff starter after Friday’s loss to Wisconsin, but both netminders will enter the postseason on a negative note after the regular season’s final series.

Still, the team has full confidence in the tandem’s ability throughout a potential playoff run, one that will start on Wednesday when the Nittany Lions host Minnesota in the opening round of the Big Ten Tournament.

“Those guys are our backbone,” Schoen said. “Both of them have been terrific all season; we left them both out to dry both nights. None of it’s on them. It’s all on us.”

Last season, the Nittany Lions were riding high when the postseason came around, and the momentum culminated in an appearance in the Frozen Four. This year, they entered the playoffs on a three-game losing streak, and at what seems to be the lowest point of the season so far.

“Last year we were fighting for our lives from the start of January,” defenseman Jarod Crespo said. “That taught us that every game matters so much, and this year the mentality has shifted a little bit away from that. We need to get back to that mentality.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball pitching struggles early, begins Texas Tech series with loss
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State continued to produce in the hitting department after scoring 12 runs in its lone home game against St. John’s. The Nittany Lions hit the road once again after earning their fourth win in mercy rule fashion.

The blue and white headed south to open up a three-game series against Texas Tech. After giving up six runs in the first inning, Penn State was unable to mount a comeback, ultimately dropping Game 1 of the series 15-7.

Colin Fitzgerald got the start on the bump for Penn State. The Lititz, Pennsylvania, native recorded 10 strikeouts and let up four earned runs in his last start.

However, the redshirt senior faced immediate struggles in the first inning against the Red Raiders. After the first two batters reached, third baseman Connor Shouse doubled in Kyeler Thompson to score the first run of the game.

With no outs and bases loaded, Shortstop Linkin Garcia doubled, followed by a single from Caden Ferraro, adding three more runs to the scoreboard. Matt Quintanar later added to the scoring after hitting a double down the left field line, scoring two more runners.

Fitzgerald’s outing came to a close after allowing six runs in the first inning. Mason Horwat came in to relief and ended the inning after one pitch.

Catcher Avery Smith took the six runs put up by Texas Tech personally as he opened the top half of the second inning, hitting his first collegiate career home run that went 328 feet. Three-straight outs ended any other answer the Nittany Lions had in the second.

Both sides were unable to record a hit following the homer, as it was the pitchers who stepped into the spotlight.

That was until left fielder Justin Turcovski’s hit slowly made its way into right field. The Nittany Lions were able to build off the one-out single after shortstop Preston Yaucher doubled in a run.
